Determine the ethical boundaries of the relationship, as needed.
Manage personal feelings evoked by the patient that may interfere with the effectiveness of therapeutic interactions.
Provide physical comfort before interactions.
Discuss confidentiality of shared information.
Create a climate of warmth and acceptance.
Reassure the patient of your interest in them as a person, as appropriate.
Use self-disclosure, as appropriate.
Return at the established time to demonstrate reliability and interest in the patient.
Maintain an open body posture.
Adjust the physical distance between nurse and patient, as appropriate.
Monitor the patient's non-verbal messages.
Seek clarification of non-verbal messages, as appropriate.
Respond to the patient's non-verbal messages, as appropriate.
Reflect the main ideas back to the patient in their own words.
Return conversation to the main topic, as needed.
Develop special forms of communication (e.g., pictures, other words), as needed.
Establish a mutually acceptable agreement on the time and duration of meetings.
Discuss the patient's responsibilities in the nurse-patient relationship.
Assist the patient in identifying areas of need to be addressed during meetings.
Establish boundaries of acceptable behavior during therapeutic sessions, as appropriate.
Set the time for the next interaction before ending the meeting.
Summarize the conversation at the end of the discussion.
Use the summary as a starting point for future conversations.
Identify the patient's readiness to explore identified problems and develop strategies for change.
Encourage the patient to take the time needed to express themselves.
Assist the patient in identifying feelings that compromise their ability to interact with others (e.g., anger, anxiety, hostility, or sadness).
Support the patient's efforts to interact with others positively.
Prepare for the end of the relationship, as appropriate.
Facilitate the patient's attempts to analyze experiences of the therapeutic relationship.
Show recognition of achievements during the relationship.

definicao: Using specialized knowledge to work with people seeking help in problem-solving, to empower individuals, families, groups, or institutions to achieve identified goals.
atividades: Identify the purpose of the consultation.
Collect data and identify the problem that is the focus of the consultation.
Identify and clarify the expectations of all parties involved.
Provide specialized knowledge to those seeking assistance.

definicao: Checking patient concerns, listening, and offering support, information, or teaching in response to patient concerns over the telephone.
atividades: Identify yourself by name and credentials, organization; inform the person if the call is being recorded (e.g., for quality monitoring), using voice to create a therapeutic relationship.
Inform the patient about the call process and obtain consent.
Consider cultural and socioeconomic barriers to patient response.
Obtain information about the purpose of the call (e.g., medical diagnosis, if applicable, past health history, and current treatment method).
Identify concerns about health status.
Establish the person's level of knowledge and the source of that knowledge.
Determine the patient's ability to understand teaching/instructions over the phone (e.g., hearing deficits, confusion, language barriers).
Provide means to overcome any identified barriers to learning or utilization of support system(s).
Identify the degree of family support and involvement in care.
Inquire about related complaints/symptoms according to standard protocol, if available.
Obtain data related to the effectiveness of current treatment(s), if any, consulting and citing approved references as sources (e.g., 'The American Red Cross suggests').
Determine the psychological response to the situation and the availability of support system(s).
Determine the safety risk of the person and others.
Determine if concerns require further assessment (use standard protocol).
Provide clear guidance on how to access necessary care if concerns are acute.
Provide information on the treatment regimen and resulting self-care responsibilities, as needed, according to scope of practice and established guidelines.
Provide information about prescribed therapies and medications, as appropriate.
Provide information on health education/health promotion, as appropriate.
Identify actual/potential problems related to implementation of the self-care regimen.
Make recommendations for changes to the regimen, as appropriate (using established guidelines, if available).
Consult with the physician/primary care provider regarding changes to the treatment regimen, as needed.
Provide information on community resources, educational programs, support groups, and self-help groups, as indicated.
Provide services in a caring and supportive manner.
Involve family/significant other(s) in care and planning.
Answer questions.
Determine the person's understanding of the information provided.
Maintain confidentiality, as indicated.
Document any assessments, advice, guidance, or other information given to the patient, according to specific guidelines.
Follow guidelines for investigation or reporting of suspected child, elder, or intimate partner abuse situations.
Follow up to determine disposition; document the patient's disposition and intended action(s).
Determine the need and establish time frames for additional intermittent assessments, as appropriate.
Determine how the patient or family member can be contacted for return phone calls, as appropriate.
Document permission for return phone calls and identify persons eligible to receive information by phone.
Discuss and resolve problematic calls with the help of a supervisor/group.

definicao: Controlling and facilitating efficient and effective use of resources.
atividades: Use supplies and equipment efficiently and effectively.
Document current or previously used resources.
Determine the appropriate healthcare setting (e.g., home care, urgent care, emergency department, clinic, acute care, long-term care) and necessary to provide services.
Assign staff within budget according to patient acuity.
Communicate and coordinate patient care needs with other departments so that care is provided in a timely manner.
Evaluate the need for healthcare attention (e.g., procedures, laboratory tests, specialty care).
Consult and negotiate with other disciplines to avoid unnecessary/duplicate tests and procedures.
Discharge the patient as soon as possible.
Investigate competitive pricing for supplies and equipment.
Determine whether supplies should be disposable or reusable.
Determine whether supplies should be purchased or rented.
Ensure supplies and equipment are competitively priced.
Use standardized documentation (e.g., graphic representation of

definicao: Application, monitoring, and removal of mechanical restraint devices or manual restraints used to limit the patient's physical mobility.
atividades: Obtain a medical order or consult with a physician within 1 hour of initiating restraint.
Recheck physical restraint orders according to state rules and regulations and professional care standards.
Ensure a face-to-face evaluation by an accredited provider within 1 hour of initiating physical restraints.
Assess the need for restraint every hour.
Provide the patient with a private, though adequately supervised, environment in situations where the patient's sense of dignity may be diminished by the use of physical restraints.
Provide sufficient personnel to assist with the safe application of physical restraint devices or manual restraints.
Designate a nursing staff member to direct the team and communicate with the patient during physical restraint application.
Use appropriate force when manually restraining the patient in emergency situations or during transport.
Instruct the patient and significant others about the behaviors that necessitated the intervention.
Instruct the patient and significant others in an understandable and non-punitive manner about the procedure, purpose, and time frame of the intervention.
Instruct the patient and significant others about the behaviors necessary to discontinue the intervention.
Monitor the patient's response to the procedure.
Do not attach restraints to bed side rails.
Secure restraints out of the patient's reach.
Provide appropriate supervision to monitor the patient and allow therapeutic actions, if needed.
Provide psychological comfort to the patient as needed.
Provide diversion activities (e.g., television, reading to the patient, visits, screens) to facilitate patient cooperation with the intervention, as appropriate.
Administer medications as needed for anxiety or agitation.
Monitor the condition of the skin at restraint sites.
Frequently monitor the color, temperature, and sensation of restrained extremities.
Allow movement and exercise according to the patient's level of self-control, clinical condition, and abilities.
Position the patient to facilitate comfort, prevent aspiration, and prevent skin injury.
Provide for movement of extremities in patients with multiple restraints, rotate restraints, and reapply one restraint at a time, as safety permits.
Assist with periodic body position changes.
Provide the dependent patient with a means to call for help (e.g., call bell or light) when the attendant is not present.
Assist with nutrition, elimination, hydration, and personal hygiene needs.
Assess, at regular intervals, the patient's need for continued restrictive intervention.
Involve the patient in activities to improve strength, coordination, judgment, and orientation.
Involve the patient in decision-making to assess the possibility of modifying the restraint to a more or less restrictive pattern, as appropriate.
Remove restraints gradually (i.e., one at a time if there are four-point restraints) as self-control increases.
Monitor the patient's response to restraint removal.
Debrief the patient and staff about the circumstances leading to the use of the intervention, as well as any patient concerns about the intervention, at the end of the restraint intervention.
Provide the next appropriate level of restrictive action (area restriction or seclusion), as needed.
Implement alternatives to restraints, such as sitting in a geriatric chair or close supervision.
Inform the family about the risks and benefits of restraints and about restraint reduction.

Document the rationale for using restraint interventions, the patient's response to the intervention, the patient's physical condition, nursing care provided throughout the intervention, and the rationale for discontinuing the intervention.

definicao: Administration, monitoring, and discontinuation of psychotropic medications used for extreme behavior control of an individual.
atividades: Implement alternative interventions to try to eliminate the need for restraint.
Provide recreational activities before using restraints (e.g., television, visitors).
Identify to the patient and significant others the behaviors that required intervention (e.g., agitation, violence).
Explain the procedure, purpose, and duration of the intervention to the patient and significant others in understandable terms.
Follow the five rights of medication administration.
Observe the patient's health history and allergies.

definicao: Negotiating an agreement with an individual that reinforces a specific behavior change.
atividades: Determine the individual's mental and cognitive capacity to engage in this negotiation.
Encourage the individual to identify their strengths and abilities.
Assist the individual in identifying health practices they wish to change.
Identify with the individual the care goals.
Encourage the individual to identify their personal goals, not those they believe the healthcare professional expects.
Avoid focusing solely on the diagnosis or pathological process when helping the person identify their goals.
Assist the individual in identifying realistic and achievable goals.
Assist the individual in identifying appropriate short- and long-term goals.
Encourage the individual to write their own goals, if possible.
State goals as easily observed behaviors.
State goals in positive terms.
Assist the individual in breaking complex goals into smaller, more manageable steps.
Clarify with the individual the respective roles of the healthcare professional and the individual.
Explore with the individual ways to best achieve the goals.
Assist the individual in examining available resources to achieve the goals.
Assist the individual in developing a plan to achieve the goals.
Assist the individual in identifying methods to overcome environmental circumstances that may interfere with achieving the goals.
Assist the individual in identifying methods to overcome environmental circumstances that may interfere with achieving the goals.
Explore with the individual methods for evaluating achieved goals.
Promote an open and accepting environment for negotiating criteria.
Facilitate the involvement of significant others in the negotiation process, if desired by the individual.
Facilitate written documentation of the negotiation, including all agreed-upon elements.
Assist the individual in establishing the time and frequency needed for performing behaviors/actions.
Assist the individual in establishing realistic time frames.
Identify a target date for contract completion with the individual.
Coordinate with the individual opportunities for review of the negotiation and goals.
Facilitate renegotiation of contract terms, if needed.
Assist the individual in discussing their feelings about the negotiation.
Observe for signs of incongruence in the individual that would indicate a lack of commitment to fulfilling the contract.
Identify with the individual the consequences or sanctions if the contract is not fulfilled, if desired.
Have the contract signed by all parties involved.
Provide a signed and dated copy of the contract to the individual.
Encourage the individual to identify meaningful and appropriate reinforcers or rewards.
Encourage the individual to choose a reinforcer or reward significant enough to maintain the behavior.
Specify with the individual when reinforcers or rewards should be given.
Identify additional rewards with the individual if goals were exceeded, if desired.
Instruct the individual on various methods of observing and recording behaviors.
Assist the individual in developing some form of chart to help track progress toward goals.
Assist the individual in recognizing even small successes.
Explore with the individual the reasons for success or failure.

definicao: Promotion of acid-base balance and prevention of complications resulting from acid-base imbalance.
atividades: Maintain a clear airway.
Position to facilitate adequate ventilation (e.g., open airway and elevate head of bed).
Maintain patent IV access.

Monitor trends in arterial pH, PaCO2, and HCO3 to verify the specific type of imbalance (e.g., respiratory or metabolic) and the presence of physiological compensation mechanisms (e.g., pulmonary or renal compensation, physiological buffers).
Maintain simultaneous assessments of arterial pH and plasma electrolytes for accurate treatment planning.
Monitor arterial blood gases and serum and urine electrolyte levels, as appropriate.
Obtain requested samples for laboratory analysis of acid-base balance (e.g., blood for arterial blood gases, urine, and serum), as appropriate.

definicao: Promotion of acid-base balance and prevention of complications resulting from serum HCO3 levels lower than desired or serum hydrogen ion levels higher than desired.
atividades: Maintain a clear airway.
Monitor respiratory pattern.
Maintain patent IV access.
Monitor possible etiologies before attempting to treat acid-base imbalances, as it is more effective to treat the etiology than the imbalance.
Differentiate pathologies that require direct intervention from those that require supportive care.

Monitor causes of HCO3 deficit or hydrogen ion excess (e.g., methanol or ethanol ingestion, uremia, diabetic ketoacidosis, alcoholic ketoacidosis, lactic acidosis, sepsis, hypotension, hypoxia, ischemia, isoniazid or iron ingestion, salicylate toxicity, diarrhea, hyperalimentation, hyperparathyroidism).
Calculate the anion gap to assist in identifying causes of metabolic acidosis (e.g., absence of anion gap indicates causes influenced by electrolytes; anion gap indicates causes of bicarbonate loss).

Use mnemonics to assist in identifying causes of metabolic acidosis (e.g., MUDPILES: Methanol ingestion, Uremia, Diabetic ketoacidosis, alcoholic or starvation ketoacidosis; Para-aldehyde ingestion; Isoniazid or iron poisoning, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol ingestion, Salicylate ingestion; HARDUP: Hyperalimentation, Acetazolamide, Renal tubular acidosis, Diarrhea and diuretics, Ureteroenterostomy, Pancreatic fistula).
Monitor electrolyte imbalances associated with metabolic acidosis (e.g., hyponatremia, hyperkalemia or hypokalemia, hypocalcemia, hypophosphatemia, and hypomagnesemia), as appropriate.

Monitor signs and symptoms of worsening HCO3 deficit or hydrogen ion excess (e.g., Kussmaul-Kien breathing, weakness, disorientation, headache, anorexia, coma, urine pH less than 6, plasma HCO3 level less than 22 mEq/L, plasma pH less than 7.35, base excess less than -2 mEq/L, associated hyperkalemia, and possible CO2 deficit).
Administer fluids as indicated for excessive losses due to underlying condition (e.g., diarrhea, diuretics, hyperalimentation).
Administer oral or parenteral HCO3 agents, if appropriate.
Use parenteral HCO3 agents cautiously in premature infants, newborns, and young children.
Avoid administering medications that result in low HCO3 levels (e.g., chloride-containing solutions and anion exchange resins), as appropriate.

Prevent complications from excessive HCO3 administration (e.g., metabolic alkalosis, hypernatremia, volume overload, decreased oxygen delivery, decreased cardiac contractility, and increased lactic acid production).
Administer prescribed insulin, hydration (isotonic and hypotonic), and potassium for the treatment of diabetic ketoacidosis, as appropriate.
Administer prescribed medications for the treatment of inappropriate substance ingestion (e.g., alcohol, salicylate, ethylene glycol) or renal insufficiency.
Monitor intake and output.
Monitor determinants of tissue oxygen delivery (e.g., PaO2, SaO2, hemoglobin levels, and cardiac output), if available.
Reduce oxygen consumption (e.g., promote comfort, control fever, and reduce anxiety), as appropriate.
Monitor bicarbonate loss from the gastrointestinal tract (e.g., diarrhea, pancreatic fistula, small bowel fistula, and ileal conduit), as appropriate.
Monitor decreased bicarbonate and acid accumulation from excess non-volatile acids (e.g., renal failure, diabetic ketoacidosis, tissue hypoxia, and starvation), as appropriate.

definicao: Promotion of acid-base balance and prevention of complications resulting from serum Paco2 or hydrogen ion levels higher than desired.
atividades: Maintain a clear airway.
Maintain clear airways (e.g., suctioning, insertion or maintenance of artificial airway, chest physiotherapy, and deep breathing cough), as appropriate.
Monitor respiratory pattern.
Maintain patent IV access.
Obtain requested samples for laboratory analysis of acid-base balance (e.g., blood for arterial blood gases, urine, and serum), as appropriate.
Monitor possible etiologies before attempting to treat acid-base imbalances, as it is more effective to treat the etiology than the imbalance.

Monitor possible causes of carbonic acid excess and respiratory acidosis (e.g., airway obstruction, ventilatory depression, CNS depression, neurological disease, chronic lung disease, musculoskeletal disease, chest trauma, pneumothorax, respiratory infection, ARDS, heart failure, acute opioid ingestion, use of respiratory depressants, obesity hypoventilation syndrome).
Differentiate pathologies that require direct intervention from those that require supportive care.

Monitor signs and symptoms of carbonic acid excess and respiratory acidosis (e.g., hand tremor extending to arms, confusion, drowsiness progressing to coma, headache, decreased verbal response, nausea, vomiting, tachycardia, warm and moist extremities, pH less than 7.35, PaCO2 greater than 45 mmHg, associated hypochloremia, and possible HCO3 excess).
Ensure ventilatory support and airway patency in the presence of respiratory acidosis and progressive increases in PaCO2, as appropriate.
Administer oxygen therapy, as appropriate.
Administer antimicrobial agents and bronchodilators, as appropriate.
Administer medication therapy aimed at reversing undesirable effects of sedatives (e.g., naloxone to reverse narcotics, flumazenil to reverse benzodiazepines), as appropriate.
Use caution when reversing the effects of benzodiazepines to avoid seizures if reversal is done too vigorously.
Administer low-flow oxygen and monitor for CO2 narcosis in cases of chronic hypercapnia (e.g., COPD).
Administer non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (e.g., nasal CPAP, BIPAP) for hypercapnia related to obesity hypoventilation syndrome or musculoskeletal diseases.

Monitor hypoventilation and treat causes (e.g., mechanical ventilation with low minute volume, chronic reduction in alveolar ventilation, COPD, acute opioid ingestion, obstructive or restrictive airway diseases).
Monitor arterial blood gases for decreased pH, as appropriate.
Monitor signs of chronic respiratory acidosis (e.g., barrel chest, digital clubbing, pursed-lip breathing, and use of accessory muscles), as appropriate.
Monitor determinants of tissue oxygen delivery (e.g., PaO2, SaO2, hemoglobin levels, cardiac output) to assess the adequacy of arterial oxygenation.
Monitor symptoms of respiratory failure (e.g., low PaO2, elevated PaCO2 levels, fatigue of respiratory muscles).
Position the patient for optimal ventilation-perfusion matching (e.g., good lung down, prone, semi-Fowler's), as appropriate.
Monitor work of breathing (e.g., respiratory rate, heart rate, use of accessory muscles, diaphoresis).
Provide mechanical ventilatory support, if needed.
Provide a low-carbohydrate, high-fat diet to reduce CO2 production, if indicated.
Provide frequent oral hygiene.
Monitor gastrointestinal function and distension to prevent reduced diaphragmatic movement, as appropriate.
Promote adequate rest periods (e.g., 90 minutes of uninterrupted sleep, organize nursing care, limit visitors, coordinate consultations), as appropriate.
Monitor neurological status (e.g., level of consciousness and confusion).
Instruct the patient and/or family on actions instituted to treat respiratory acidosis.
Coordinate limited visiting hours with the patient's visitors to allow sufficient rest periods to reduce respiratory compromise, if indicated.

definicao: Promotion of acid-base balance and prevention of complications resulting from serum HCO3 or pH levels higher than desired.
atividades: Prepare the patient with renal failure for dialysis (i.e., assist in passing the dialysis catheter), as appropriate.
Assist with dialysis (e.g., hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis), as appropriate.
Instruct on seizure precautions.
Provide frequent oral hygiene.
Maintain bed rest, as indicated.
Monitor manifestations of worsening metabolic acidosis on the CNS (e.g., headache, dizziness, decreased mental activity, seizures, and coma), as appropriate.
Monitor cardiopulmonary manifestations of worsening metabolic acidosis (e.g., hypotension, hypoxia, arrhythmias, and Kussmaul-Kien breathing), as appropriate.
Monitor gastrointestinal manifestations of worsening metabolic acidosis (e.g., anorexia, nausea, and vomiting), as appropriate.
Provide adequate nutrition to patients with chronic metabolic acidosis.
Provide comfort measures to manage the gastrointestinal effects of metabolic acidosis.
Encourage a low-carbohydrate diet to decrease CO2 production (e.g., administration of hyperalimentation and total parenteral nutrition), as appropriate.
Monitor calcium and phosphate levels for patients with chronic metabolic acidosis to prevent bone loss.
Instruct the patient and/or family on actions instituted to treat metabolic acidosis.

definicao: Promotion of acid-base balance and prevention of complications resulting from serum Paco2 levels lower than desired.
atividades: Maintain a clear airway.
Monitor respiratory pattern.
Maintain patent IV access.
Monitor possible etiologies before attempting to treat acid-base imbalances, as it is more effective to treat the etiology than the imbalance.
Differentiate pathologies that require direct intervention from those that require supportive care.

Monitor hyperventilation and treat causes (e.g., mechanical ventilation with high minute volume, anxiety, hypoxemia, lung injuries, severe anemia, salicylate toxicity, CNS injury, hypermetabolic states, gastrointestinal distension, pain, high altitude, septicemia, stress).
Reduce oxygen consumption by promoting comfort, controlling fever, and reducing anxiety to minimize hyperventilation, as appropriate.
Provide a rebreathing mask for the hyperventilating patient, as appropriate.
Sedate the patient to reduce hyperventilation, if appropriate.

definicao: Promoting adequate ventilation and tissue perfusion for an individual with a severe allergic (antigen-antibody) reaction.
atividades: Identify and remove the source of the allergen, if possible.
Administer epinephrine 1:1,000 subcutaneously in an age-appropriate dose.
Place the individual in a comfortable position.
Apply a tourniquet per protocol immediately proximal to the allergen entry point (e.g., injection site, IV access, insect sting), when possible, as appropriate.
Establish and maintain a clear airway.
Administer high-flow oxygen (10 to 15 L/min).
Monitor vital signs.
Start IV infusion of normal saline, lactated Ringer's, or plasma expander, as appropriate.
Reassure the individual and family.
Monitor for signs of shock (e.g., respiratory distress, cardiac arrhythmias, seizures, and hypotension).
Monitor self-reports of impending sense of doom.
Maintain a record of activities on appropriate form, including vital signs and medication administration.
Administer IV fluids rapidly (1,000 mL/hour) to maintain blood pressure, as prescribed or per protocol.
Administer antispasmodics, antihistamines, or corticosteroids in the presence of urticaria, angioedema, or bronchospasm.
Consult and refer to other healthcare providers, as needed.
Monitor for recurrence of anaphylaxis for 24 hours.
Instruct the individual and family on the use of an epinephrine pen.
Instruct the individual and family on preventing future episodes.

definicao: Preparation and administration of anesthetic agents and monitoring of patient response during administration.
atividades: Assess the patient preoperatively, preferably one or more days before the procedure.
Verify that the information provided by the patient matches the identification device (e.g., wristbands, bed labels, fingerprint recognition software, palm vein scanner) and the medical record.
Assess the patient and record to identify pre-existing conditions, allergies, and contraindications for specific techniques or anesthetics.
Document patient preparation for anesthesia (e.g., preoperative fasting, medication schedule, absence of upper respiratory tract infections, airway history).
Document the patient's weight.

definicao: Identification, treatment, and prevention of reactions to airway inflammation/constriction.
atividades: Assess initial respiratory status for use as a baseline.
Document baseline measurements in the chart.
Compare current status with previous status to detect changes in respiratory status.
Obtain spirometry measurements (FEV1, FVC, FEV1/FVC ratio) before and after use of a short-acting bronchodilator.
Monitor peak expiratory flow (PEF), as appropriate.
Teach the patient about using a peak flow meter (PFM) at home.
Monitor asthmatic reactions.
Determine patient/family understanding of the disease and its management.
Instruct patient/family about anti-inflammatory and bronchodilator medications and their appropriate use.
Teach proper techniques for using medications and equipment (e.g., inhaler, nebulizer, PFM).
Verify adherence to prescribed treatments.
Encourage verbalization of feelings about the diagnosis, treatment, and impact on lifestyle.
Identify known triggers and the usual reaction.
Teach the patient to identify and avoid triggers, if possible.
Establish a written plan with the patient to manage exacerbations.
Assist in recognizing signs/symptoms of an impending asthmatic reaction and implementing appropriate response measures.
Monitor the rate, rhythm, depth, and effort of breathing.
Observe the onset, characteristics, and duration of cough.
Observe chest movement, including symmetry, use of accessory muscles, and retraction of supraclavicular and intercostal muscles.
Auscultate breath sounds, noting areas of decreased/absent ventilation and adventitious sounds.
Administer medications, as appropriate, and/or according to guidelines or protocols.
Auscultate lung sounds after treatment to verify results.
Offer warm drinks, as appropriate.
Teach breathing/relaxation techniques.
Use a calm, reassuring approach during an asthma attack.
Inform patient/family about policies and procedures for carrying and administering asthma medications at school.
Inform parents/guardians when the child needs or uses prescribed 'as needed' medications at school, as appropriate.
Refer for medical evaluation, as appropriate.
Establish a regular follow-up care program.
Instruct and monitor school staff on emergency procedures.
Prescribe and/or renew asthma medications, as appropriate.

definicao: Provision of a modified environment for the patient in a state of chronic confusion.
atividades: Include family members in planning, implementing, and evaluating care, as desired.
Identify habitual patterns of behavior for activities such as sleep, medication use, elimination, food intake, and self-care.
Assess the patient's physical, social, and psychological history, habits, and routines.
Assess the type and degree of cognitive deficit(s) using a standardized assessment tool.
Monitor cognitive function using a standardized assessment tool.
Determine appropriate behavioral expectations for the patient's cognitive state.

Provide a low-stimulation environment (e.g., calm, relaxing music; neutral, simple, and familiar patterns of decoration; performance expectations that do not exceed cognitive processing capacity; and meals in small groups).
Provide adequate lighting without glare.
Identify and eliminate potential environmental hazards for the patient.
Place an identification bracelet on the patient.
Provide a consistent physical environment and daily routine.
Prepare for interaction with eye contact and touch, as appropriate.
Introduce yourself when initiating contact.
Call the patient distinctly by name when initiating interaction and speak slowly.
Provide one simple instruction at a time.
Speak in a clear, low, friendly, and respectful tone of voice.
Use distraction rather than confrontation to manage behavior.
Show unconditional positive interest.
Avoid touch and closeness if it causes stress or anxiety.
Assign caregivers who are familiar to the patient (e.g., avoid frequent staff changes).
Avoid unfamiliar situations (e.g., room changes and appointments without family present), when possible.
Provide rest periods to prevent fatigue and reduce stress.
Monitor eating and weight.
Provide safe pacing and ambulation.
Avoid frustrating the patient by asking orientation questions that cannot be answered.
Provide cues, such as current events, seasons, location, and names – to assist with orientation.
Seat the patient at a small table in groups of three to five for meals, as appropriate.
Allow the patient to eat alone, as appropriate.
Provide snacks to maintain nutrition for the patient who will not sit down to eat.
Provide the patient with general orientation to the season using appropriate cues (e.g., holiday decorations, seasonal decorations and activities, and access to fenced outdoor areas).
Decrease noise levels, avoiding beeping information systems and alert lights.
Select television or radio activities based on interests and cognitive processing abilities.
Select individual and group activities tailored to the patient's cognitive abilities and interests.
Label familiar photos with the names of individuals in them.
Select artwork for the patient's room featuring familiar landscapes, scenes, or other images.
Ask family and friends to visit the patient one or two at a time to reduce stimulation if needed.
Discuss with family and friends the best way to interact with the patient.
Assist the family in understanding that it may be impossible for the patient to learn something new.
Limit the number of choices the patient has to make to avoid provoking anxiety.
Set boundaries, such as red or yellow tape on the floor when low-stimulation units are not available.

definicao: Reduction of aggressive behavior during body cleaning.
atividades: Personalize the bath according to the patient's usual bathing preferences and/or cultural traditions.
Use a flexible approach, offering choices and control over time of day and type of bath (shower, tub, or sponge bath).
Avoid the terms 'bath' and 'shower' if possible to reduce anxiety.
Ensure privacy and safety while the patient is undressing and bathing.
Simulate the home environment as much as possible (e.g., wall decor, soft music, aromatherapy, soft lighting).
Provide a comfortable environment (e.g., temperature, lighting, noise reduction).
Give a reason for the bath (e.g., 'Let's get you cleaned up before your daughter arrives').
Avoid rapid transport to the bathroom.
Start the bath slowly, first letting water drip on the hand.
Take time to perform care without rushing.
Undress the patient gradually in the bathroom while discussing something of interest other than the bath.
Use familiar bathing products to promote relaxation.
Ensure the water is at the proper temperature.
Reduce the feeling of cold by offering warmed towels, washing feet first, and saving hair for last, or having hair washed by a hairdresser.
Place a warm towel on the head and shoulders while washing lower extremities.
Massage the skin with calming lotion after the bath.
View the patient as a whole, focusing on the person rather than the task.
Assign a trusted caregiver with a friendly attitude to the task.
Respond according to the patient's perceptions (e.g., temperature, pain, and fear of drowning).
Use gentle persuasion, not coercion.
Use a soft, reassuring tone of voice.
Discuss topics of interest to the patient with a calm, pleasant approach.
Use gentle touch.
Give short, simple instructions.
Encourage the patient to assist with the bath according to their ability.
Use distraction rather than confrontation to manage behavior.
Maintain a calm, quiet environment.
Assign a same-gender caregiver, if available.
Identify antecedents or 'triggers' if aggressive behavior occurs.
Monitor verbal and non-verbal signs of increased agitation.
Give analgesic medication before bathing if movements are painful.
Offer a sponge bath if other methods cause agitation.
Remove dentures or offer something to eat to prevent biting during bathing.
Provide a towel or something to hold during bathing.
Use comfortable bathing equipment.

definicao: Provision of care to a patient who walks back and forth repeatedly and returns, with attempts to elope or who gets lost when unaccompanied.
atividades: Include family members in planning, implementing, and evaluating care, as desired.
Identify habitual patterns of wandering behavior.
Identify and eliminate potential environmental hazards for the patient.
Modify unsafe aspects of the patient's home (i.e., remove rugs, label rooms, and keep the home well-lit).
Alert neighbors about the patient's wandering behavior.
Alert the police and take current photos of the patient.
Place a medical alert bracelet or necklace on the patient.

Provide a low-stimulation environment (e.g., quietness, soft music; familiar, simple patterns without bright colors in decoration; performance expectations that do not exceed cognitive processing capacity; meals in small groups).
Carefully monitor for physiological causes of increased confusion that may be acute and reversible.
Monitor nutrition and weight.
Monitor interactions, side effects, and desired therapeutic effects of medications.
Recommend medication adjustments as needed.
Ensure the patient has adequate hydration.
Place the patient's name in large letters on the room and on clothing, as needed.
Discuss home safety issues and interventions.
Install complex safety locks on doors leading outside or to the basement.
Install alarm devices and sensors on doors.
Use electronic technological devices to locate and monitor wandering (e.g., GPS, radio frequency identification).
Set boundaries, such as red or yellow tape on the floor when low-stimulation units are not available.
Use symbols, not written signs, to help the patient locate the room, bathroom, or other areas.
Install safety bars in the bathroom.
Provide a consistent physical environment and daily routine.
Provide a safe place for wandering.

definicao: Management and relief of diarrhea.
atividades: Assess history of diarrhea.
Obtain stool for culture and sensitivity if diarrhea continues.
Review routinely used medications for gastrointestinal side effects.
Teach the patient about proper use of antidiarrheal medications.
Instruct patient/family to record the color, volume, frequency, and consistency of stools.
Assess intake records for nutritional content.
Encourage small, frequent meals, adding bulk gradually.
Teach the patient to eliminate spicy and gas-forming foods from the diet.
Suggest eliminating lactose-containing foods.
Identify factors (e.g., medications, bacteria, tube feedings) that may cause or contribute to diarrhea.
Monitor signs and symptoms of diarrhea.
Instruct the patient to notify staff of each diarrhea episode.
Observe skin turgor regularly.
Monitor irritation and ulceration of the perianal area.
Measure the amount of diarrhea/stool output.
Weigh the patient regularly.
Notify the physician of increased frequency or intensity of bowel sounds.
Consult the physician if signs and symptoms of diarrhea persist.
Instruct on a low-fiber, high-protein, high-calorie diet, as appropriate.
Instruct the patient to avoid laxatives.
Teach patient/family to keep a food diary.
Teach the patient stress reduction techniques, as appropriate.
Assist the patient in performing stress reduction techniques.
Monitor safe food preparation.
Take actions that provide bowel rest (e.g., NPO, clear liquid diet).

definicao: Prevention and elimination of stimuli that cause hyperactive reflexes and inappropriate autonomic reactions in a patient with cervical or high thoracic spinal cord injury.
atividades: Identify and minimize stimuli capable of precipitating dysreflexia (e.g., bladder distension, renal calculi, infection, fecal impaction, rectal examination, suppository insertion, skin breakdown, and tight clothing or bed linens).

Monitor signs and symptoms of autonomic dysreflexia (e.g., paroxysmal hypertension, bradycardia, tachycardia, diaphoresis above the level of injury, facial flushing, pallor below the level of injury, headache, nasal congestion, engorgement of temporal and cervical vessels, conjunctival congestion, chills without fever, piloerection, and chest pain).
Investigate and remove the cause (e.g., distended bladder, fecal impaction, skin lesions, tight bed linens, elastic stockings, and abdominal binders).
Elevate the head of the bed to lower blood pressure and promote cerebral venous return, as appropriate.
Stay with the patient and monitor their status every 3 to 5 minutes if hyperreflexia occurs.
Administer IV antihypertensives as prescribed.
Instruct the patient and family on causes, symptoms, treatment, and prevention of dysreflexia.

definicao: Maintenance of an excellent pattern of urinary elimination.
atividades: Monitor urinary elimination including frequency, consistency, odor, volume, and color, as appropriate.
Monitor for signs and symptoms of urinary retention.
Identify factors contributing to incontinence episodes.
Instruct the patient about signs and symptoms of urinary tract infection.
Note the time of the last urinary elimination, as appropriate.
Instruct patient/family to record urinary output, as appropriate.
Insert urethral suppository, if appropriate.
Obtain a clean-catch urine specimen for analysis, as appropriate.
Refer to the physician in the presence of signs and symptoms of urinary tract

definicao: Prevention and treatment of blood glucose levels above normal.
atividades: Monitor blood glucose levels, as indicated.
Monitor signs and symptoms of hyperglycemia: polyuria, polydipsia, polyphagia, weakness, lethargy, malaise, blurred vision, or headache.
Monitor urine ketones, as indicated.
Monitor blood gases, electrolytes, and beta-hydroxybutyrate levels, as available.
Monitor orthostatic blood pressure and pulse, as indicated.
Administer insulin as prescribed.
Encourage oral fluid intake.
Monitor fluid status (including inputs and outputs), as appropriate.
Maintain IV access, as appropriate.
Administer IV fluids, as appropriate.
Administer potassium, as prescribed.
Consult the physician if signs and symptoms of hyperglycemia persist or worsen.
Assist with ambulation if orthostatic hypotension is present.
Provide oral hygiene, if needed.
Identify possible causes of hyperglycemia.
Anticipate situations where insulin requirement will increase (e.g., intercurrent illness).
Restrict exercise when glucose levels are above 250 mg/dL, especially if urine ketones are present.
Instruct the patient and significant others about prevention, recognition, and management of hyperglycemia.
Encourage self-monitoring of blood glucose levels.
Assist the patient in interpreting blood glucose levels.
Review blood glucose records with the patient and/or family.
Instruct on urine ketone testing, as appropriate.
Instruct on indications and significance of urine ketone testing, if appropriate.
Instruct the patient to report moderate or high urine ketone levels to the healthcare provider.

Instruct the patient and significant others in managing diabetes during illness, including use of insulin and/or oral agents, monitoring fluid intake, carbohydrate replacement, and when to seek healthcare provider assistance, as appropriate.
Provide assistance in regimen adjustment to prevent and treat hyperglycemia (e.g., increasing insulin or oral agent) as indicated.
Facilitate adherence to diet and exercise regimen.
Test glucose levels of family members.

definicao: Prevention and treatment of blood cholesterol and triglyceride levels higher than normal.
atividades: Obtain a detailed patient health history to determine the presence of hyperlipidemia or the patient's risk level, including current medications and laboratory values.

Identify possible causes of hyperlipidemia (e.g., diet high in saturated fats, red meats, fried foods, dairy, low-fiber diet; smoking; obesity; sedentary lifestyle; family history of high cholesterol levels), including metabolic pathologies and medications that affect lipid metabolism (e.g., hypothyroidism, liver or kidney disease, anorexia nervosa, untreated diabetes, glucocorticoids, estrogens).
Perform a thorough physical examination, including vital signs, height, weight, waist-to-hip ratio, and body mass index (BMI).

Identify patients at risk (e.g., diet high in saturated fats, red meats, fried foods, dairy, and low fiber; obesity; sedentary lifestyle; family history of high cholesterol levels; smoking or exposure to smoke).
Use the National Cholesterol Education Expert Adult Treatment Panel III guidelines to determine risk categories and treatment options.
Determine the patient's risk level.
Apply treatment guidelines to determine treatment goals (e.g., lifestyle changes as preventive measures versus lifestyle changes with medications).

Counsel high-risk patients to practice lifestyle modification to reduce their risk of developing hyperlipidemia (e.g., increase exercise, lose weight, modify diet, avoid or stop smoking, avoid excessive alcohol consumption).
Counsel at-risk patients to seek appropriate medication therapy if lifestyle modification attempts fail to reduce hyperlipidemia to risk levels.
Assist patients with hyperlipidemia in practicing lifestyle modifications and using appropriate medication therapy as indicated by treatment guidelines.
Monitor blood chemistry, including cholesterol, triglycerides, LDL, and HDL, as indicated by treatment guidelines.
Monitor weight and waist-to-hip ratio.
Instruct at-risk patients to undergo regular preventive health assessments, including cholesterol levels.
Encourage the patient to follow the American Heart Association recommendation for cholesterol screening every 4 to 6 years, starting at age 20.
Instruct on a healthy dietary pattern (e.g., intake

definicao: Prevention and treatment of blood pressure levels above normal.
atividades: Obtain a detailed patient health history to determine risk level, including current medications.
Identify possible causes of hypertension.

Assess associated risk factors and contributing factors (e.g., diabetes mellitus, dyslipidemia, obesity, metabolic syndrome, age over 60 years, gender, race, smoking, hyperuricemia, sedentary lifestyle, family history of hypertension, cardiovascular disease, history of stroke).

Measure blood pressure (BP) to determine the presence of hypertension (e.g., normal, below 120/80; elevated, 120 to 129/80 or less; stage 1 hypertension, 130 to 139/80 to 89; stage 2 hypertension, equal to or above 140/90).
Ensure proper blood pressure assessment (e.g., classification is based on the average of two or more proper readings, in a seated position, blood pressure readings at two or more visits).
Avoid taking blood pressure for classification when contributing factors are present (e.g., caffeine consumption, migraine attack, insomnia, agitation).
Implement appropriate nursing care for patients based on hypertension classification.
Assist prehypertensive patients in practicing lifestyle modification to reduce their future risk of developing hypertension (e.g., increase exercise, lose weight, modify diet, get adequate sleep).

Counsel prehypertensive patients with comorbid conditions (e.g., heart failure, diabetes, kidney disease) to seek appropriate medication therapy if lifestyle modification fails to reduce blood pressure to 130/80 mmHg or less.

Assist patients with stage 1 hypertension and no comorbid conditions (e.g., heart failure, diabetes, kidney disease) in practicing lifestyle modifications and using appropriate medication therapy (e.g., thiazide-type diuretics for most, possibly ACE inhibitor, ARB, beta-blocker, calcium channel blocker, or combination).

Assist patients with stage 2 hypertension and no comorbid conditions (e.g., heart failure, diabetes, kidney disease) in practicing lifestyle modifications and using appropriate medication therapy (e.g., combinations of ACE inhibitor, ARB, beta-blocker, calcium channel blocker).

Assist patients with stage 1 or 2 hypertension and comorbid conditions (e.g., heart failure, diabetes, kidney disease) in practicing lifestyle modifications where possible and following recommended medication regimen protocols for comorbid conditions with hypertension.

Monitor signs and symptoms of hypertensive crisis in at-risk patients (e.g., severe headache, dizziness, nausea or vomiting, pallor, diaphoresis, cold skin, visual changes, epistaxis, confusion, nervousness, agitation, visual disturbances, altered level of consciousness, chest pain, seizures, cardiac arrest).
Monitor vital signs such as heart rate, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, temperature, and blood panels for early identification of complications.
Instruct at-risk patients to undergo regular preventive health assessments, including electrocardiogram, echocardiogram, electrolytes, urinalysis, as indicated.
Monitor signs and symptoms of hypertension or hypotension in the patient after administration of prescribed antihypertensive medication.
Instruct on a healthy dietary pattern.
Instruct on adequate physical activity (e.g., exercise for 30 to 45 minutes per day).
Instruct on contributing lifestyle habits to avoid (e.g., tobacco use in any form and alcohol).
Instruct the patient on lifestyle modification related to sleep and rest patterns (e.g., 8 hours of sleep per night is recommended).
Provide information on possible lifestyle changes needed to prevent future complications and manage the disease process.
Provide information related to the goal and benefit of lifestyle changes.
Instruct on self-monitoring of blood pressure and reporting abnormal findings.
Instruct the patient on possible causes of hypertension.

Instruct the patient and family to take an active role in managing the disease process (e.g., medication indications and administration, maintaining proper diet, exercise and healthy habits, smoking cessation, stress reduction, weight reduction, sodium intake reduction, alcohol consumption reduction, increased exercise, as indicated).
Instruct the patient and family on medication use and indications.
Encourage the patient and family to maintain a list of medications and routinely reconcile with routine check-ups, hospital visits, or

definicao: Prevention and treatment of low blood glucose levels.
atividades: Identify the patient at risk for hypoglycemia.
Determine recognition of signs and symptoms of hypoglycemia.
Monitor blood glucose levels, as indicated.

Monitor for signs and symptoms of hypoglycemia (e.g., tremor, diaphoresis, nervousness, anxiety, irritability, impatience, tachycardia, palpitations, chills, viscosity, dizziness, pallor, hunger, nausea, headache, fatigue, drowsiness, weakness, heat, feeling of fainting, blurred vision, nightmares, crying during sleep, paresthesias, difficulty concentrating, difficulty speaking, lack of coordination, behavior change, confusion, coma, seizure).
Offer simple carbohydrate as indicated.
Provide complex carbohydrate and protein as indicated.
Administer glucagon as indicated.
Contact emergency medical services as needed.
Administer intravenous glucose as indicated.
Maintain IV access as appropriate.
Maintain clear airways as needed.
Protect from injury as needed.
Review events preceding hypoglycemia to determine probable cause.
Provide a response regarding acceptance of hypoglycemia self-management.
Instruct the patient and significant others about signs and symptoms, risk factors, and treatment for hypoglycemia.
Instruct the patient to have simple carbohydrate available at all times.
Instruct the patient about obtaining and carrying/using appropriate emergency identification.
Instruct significant others about the use and administration of glucagon, as appropriate.
Instruct regarding diet, insulin/oral agent, and exercise instructions.
Provide assistance in making self-care decisions to prevent hypoglycemia (e.g., reducing insulin/oral agents and/or increasing food intake for exercise).
Encourage self-monitoring of blood glucose levels.
Encourage ongoing telephone contact with the diabetes care team for consultation regarding regimen adjustments.
Collaborate with the patient and diabetes care team to make changes to the insulin regimen (e.g., multiple daily injections), as indicated.
Modify blood glucose goals to prevent hypoglycemia in the absence of hypoglycemia symptoms.
Inform the patient about the increased risk of hypoglycemia with intensive therapy and normalization of blood glucose levels.
Instruct the patient about changes in hypoglycemia symptoms with intensive therapy and normalization of blood glucose levels.

definicao: Prevention and treatment of blood pressure levels below normal.
atividades: Obtain a detailed patient health history to determine the patient's risk level for hypotension, including current medications.

Identify possible causes of hypotension (e.g., diabetes, coronary artery disease, peripheral artery disease, heart failure, previous stroke, chronic kidney disease, proteinuria, age over 50 years, smoking, obesity, treatment of Parkinson's disease, treatment of seizure disorder, treatment of depression; nocturnal diuresis).
Measure blood pressure (BP) to determine the presence of hypotension (e.g., defined as less than 90 mmHg systolic and/or less than 60 mmHg diastolic in the general population).
Ensure proper blood pressure assessment (e.g., hypotension classification is based on the average of two or more proper readings with position change, BP readings at two or more visits).
Instruct patients to maintain a blood pressure diary with orthostatic blood pressure readings taken over several days at various times to increase sensitivity for detecting pending issues.
Perform an electrocardiogram, if applicable.
Perform biochemical analysis, if applicable.
Perform blood gas study, if applicable.
Perform additional diagnostic tests, if indicated.
Avoid taking BP for classification when contributing factors are present (e.g., recent ingestion of medications that may influence, allergic responses, blood loss, dehydration).
Obtain descriptive experiences of hypotension from the patient (e.g., occurrences, situations, causes, time of day, food or medication consumption, sleep patterns).
Determine the medication regimen and influences on hypotension (e.g., diuretics, alpha-blockers, beta-blockers, Parkinson's disease medications, tricyclic antidepressants).
Address any condition that influences hypotension as needed.
Consider non-pharmacological treatments as the first approach.
Discontinue medications that predispose to or exacerbate hypotension, as appropriate.

Instruct the patient about physical countermeasures to reduce gravitational pooling of blood in the lower extremities (e.g., moving gradually from supine to standing, avoiding standing still, standing with legs crossed, squatting, actively tensing leg muscles).

Encourage the patient to employ approaches that improve central volume (e.g., increasing salt intake to 6 to 9 g of sodium chloride per day with 1 g sodium chloride tablets with each meal if needed; increasing water intake to up to 2 to 3 L per day with rapid water ingestion [475 mL in 3 to 4 minutes] used as a rescue measure).

Monitor complications (e.g., blurred vision, confusion, dizziness, syncope, pallor, cold diaphoresis, tachycardia, lightheadedness, drowsiness, weakness, altered level of consciousness, seizure, shortness of breath, chest pain).
Be alert for the need to treat worsening symptoms.

definicao: Expansion of intravascular fluid volume in a patient whose volume is decreased.
atividades: Weigh daily at consistent times (e.g., after voiding, before breakfast) and monitor trends.

Monitor hemodynamic status, including heart rate, blood pressure, mean arterial pressure, central venous pressure, pulmonary artery pressure, pulmonary capillary wedge pressure, cardiac output, and cardiac index, if available.
Monitor for evidence of dehydration (e.g., poor skin turgor, delayed capillary refill, weak/thready pulse, severe thirst, dry mucous membranes, and decreased urinary output).
Monitor for orthostatic hypotension and dizziness upon standing.
Monitor sources of fluid loss (e.g., bleeding, vomiting, diarrhea, excessive diaphoresis, and tachypnea).
Monitor intake and output.
Monitor the vascular access device insertion site for infiltration, phlebitis, and infection, as appropriate.
Monitor laboratory evidence for blood loss (e.g., hemoglobin, hematocrit, fecal occult blood test) if available.
Monitor laboratory evidence for hemoconcentration (e.g., sodium, blood urea nitrogen, urine specific gravity), if available.

Monitor clinical and laboratory evidence for impending acute renal failure (e.g., elevated BUN, elevated creatinine, decreased glomerular filtration rate, myoglobinemia, and decreased urinary output).
Encourage the patient to drink fluids (e.g., distribute fluids over 24 hours and offer fluids with meals), unless contraindicated.
Offer a drink of choice every 1 to 2 hours while awake, unless contraindicated.
Maintain patent IV access.
Calculate fluid needs based on body surface area and burn size, as appropriate.
Administer prescribed isotonic IV solutions (e.g., normal saline or lactated Ringer's solution) for extracellular rehydration at an appropriate flow rate, as appropriate.
Administer prescribed hypotonic IV solutions (e.g., 5% dextrose in water or 0.45% sodium chloride) for intracellular rehydration at an appropriate flow rate, as appropriate.
Administer prescribed isotonic IV fluid boluses at an appropriate fluid rate to maintain hemodynamic integrity.
Administer prescribed colloid suspensions (e.g., Hespan®, albumin, or Plasmanate® – human serum protein) for intravascular volume replacement, as appropriate.
Administer prescribed blood products to increase plasma oncotic pressure and replace blood volume, as appropriate.
Monitor for evidence of transfusion reaction, as appropriate.
Institute autotransfusion of blood loss, if appropriate.
Monitor for evidence of hypervolemia and pulmonary edema during IV rehydration.
Administer IV fluids at room temperature.
Use an IV pump to maintain a constant IV infusion flow rate.
Monitor skin integrity in immobile patients with dry skin.
Promote skin integrity (e.g., prevent cuts, avoid excessive moisture, and provide adequate nutrition) in immobile patients with dry skin, as appropriate.
Assist the patient with ambulation in case of postural hypotension.
Instruct the patient to avoid rapid position changes, especially from supine to sitting or standing.

Schedule modified Trendelenburg positioning (e.g., legs elevated above heart level with the rest of the body supine) when hypotensive to optimize cerebral perfusion while minimizing myocardial oxygen demand.
Monitor the oral cavity for dry and/or cracked mucous membranes.
Provide oral fluids (or pass moistened cotton swabs in the mouth) frequently to maintain mucous membrane integrity, unless contraindicated.
Facilitate oral cleansing (e.g., toothbrush with toothpaste, alcohol-free mouthwash) twice daily.
Position for peripheral perfusion.
Administer prescribed vasodilators with caution (e.g., nitroglycerin, sodium nitroprusside, and calcium channel blockers) when warming a postoperative patient, as appropriate.
Administer atrial natriuretic peptide (ANP) for prevention of acute kidney injury, as appropriate.
Instruct the patient and/or family on the use of intake and output records, as appropriate.
Instruct the patient and/or family on measures instituted to treat hypovolemia.

definicao: Control of material, instrument, equipment, and sterility requirements of the surgical field.
atividades: Consult surgical schedules, operating room assignments, and obtain information about the surgical procedure and anesthetic technique.

definicao: Safe protection and reintegration of the affected body part, while assisting the patient to adapt to the change in perceptual ability.
atividades: Assess baseline mental status, comprehension, motor function, sensory function, attention, and affective responses.
Monitor for abnormal responses to three primary types of stimuli: sensory, visual, and auditory.
Provide realistic information about the patient's perceptual deficit.
Perform personal care consistently with a complete explanation.
Ensure that the affected extremities are positioned properly and safely.
Adapt the environment for the deficit by focusing on the unaffected side during the acute phase.
Supervise and/or assist during transfers and ambulation.
Touch the unaffected shoulder when starting conversation.
Place beverages and food within the patient's field of vision and rotate the plate when needed.
Reorganize the environment to utilize the right or left visual field, positioning personal items, television, or reading materials within the visual field on the unaffected side.
Give frequent reminders to redirect the patient's attention, with suggestions about the environment to the patient.
Avoid rapid movements within the room.
Avoid moving objects around the room.
Position the bed so that individuals approach the patient from the unaffected side.
Keep the bed rail elevated on the affected side, when appropriate.
Instruct the patient to look from left to right.
Apply massage and range-of-motion exercises to the affected side.
Encourage the patient to touch and use the affected body part.
Consult occupational therapists and physical therapists about the optimal timing and strategies to facilitate reintegration of neglected body parts and function.
Gradually focus the patient's attention on the affected side as the patient demonstrates ability to compensate for neglect.
Gradually move personal items and activities to the affected side as the patient demonstrates ability to compensate for the deficit.
Stand on the affected side when ambulating with the patient as the patient demonstrates ability to compensate for neglect.
Assist the patient with daily activities from the affected side as the patient demonstrates ability to compensate for the deficit.
Assist the patient in washing and grooming the affected side first as the patient demonstrates ability to compensate for the deficit.
Focus on tactile and verbal stimuli on the affected side as the patient demonstrates ability to compensate for the deficit.
Instruct caregivers about the cause, mechanisms, and treatment of the deficit.
Include the family in the rehabilitation process to support the patient's efforts and assist with care, when appropriate.

definicao: Assisting the patient and family in understanding the action and minimizing the side effects of antineoplastic agents.
atividades: Monitor pre-treatment screening tests for patients at risk.

definicao: Administration of sedatives, monitoring of patient response, and provision of necessary physiological support during diagnostic or therapeutic procedures.
atividades: Review the patient's health history and diagnostic test results to determine if the patient meets institutional criteria for nurse-administered conscious sedation.
Ask the patient or family about any previous experiences with conscious sedation.
Verify medication allergies.
Determine the last food and fluid intake.
Review other medications taken by the patient and check for contraindications to sedation.
Educate the patient and/or family about the effects of sedation.
Obtain written informed consent.
Assess the patient's level of consciousness and protective reflexes before administering sedation.
Obtain baseline vital signs, oxygen saturation, ECG, height, and weight.
Ensure the ready availability of emergency resuscitation equipment, specifically a source of 100% oxygen delivery, emergency medications, and a defibrillator.
Establish IV access.
Administer medication as per physician order or protocol, titrating carefully according to patient response.
Monitor the patient's level of consciousness and vital signs, oxygen saturation, and ECG, following institutional protocol.
Monitor the patient for adverse drug events.

definicao: Prevention or minimization of injury or discomfort in the patient with altered sensation.
atividades: Monitor discrimination between acute and chronic pain, heat, or cold.
Monitor for paresthesias (e.g., numbness, tingling, hyperesthesia, hypoesthesia, and pain intensity), as appropriate.
Encourage the patient to use the unaffected body part to determine the temperature of food, liquids, bath water, etc.
Encourage the patient to use the unaffected body part to identify the location and texture of objects.
Instruct the patient or family to monitor the position of body parts during bathing, while sitting, lying down, or changing position.
Instruct the patient or family to examine the skin daily for changes in integrity.
Monitor the fit of immobilizers, prostheses, footwear, and clothing.
Instruct the patient or family to use thermometers to test water temperature.
Encourage the use of thermal protectors when the patient handles kitchen utensils.

Encourage the use of gloves or other protective covering over the affected body part when it is in contact with objects that, due to their temperature, texture, or other inherent characteristic, may be potentially dangerous.
Avoid or carefully monitor the use of heat or ice, such as warm compresses, hot water bottles, or ice packs.
Encourage the patient to wear well-fitting, low-heeled, comfortable shoes.
Place a protective covering over body parts to keep bedding away from affected areas.
Check shoes, pockets, and clothing for folds or foreign objects.
Instruct the patient to use predetermined intervals for position changes rather than discomfort signals.
Use pressure-relieving devices, as appropriate.
Protect body parts from extreme temperature changes.
Immobilize head, neck, and back, as appropriate.
Monitor the ability to urinate and defecate.
Establish means to urinate, as appropriate.
Establish means to defecate, as appropriate.
Administer analgesics, corticosteroids, anticonvulsants, tricyclic antidepressants, or local anesthetics, as needed.
Monitor for thrombophlebitis and venous thromboembolism.
Discuss or identify causes of abnormal sensation or changes in sensation.
Instruct the patient to visually monitor the position of body parts if proprioception is impaired.

definicao: Relief/attenuation of physical and/or behavioral symptoms occurring during the luteal phase of the menstrual cycle.
atividades: Instruct the patient on prospective identification of major premenstrual symptoms (e.g., bloating, cramps, irritability), use of a prospective calendar or symptom chart, and recording the onset and intensity of each symptom.
Review the symptom list.
Collaborate with the woman in prioritizing the most problematic symptoms.
Discuss the complexity of treatment and the need for a stepwise approach to symptom relief.
Collaborate with the woman to choose and institute a stepwise approach to eliminate symptoms.
Provide information on specific self-care measures for symptoms (e.g., exercise and calcium supplementation).
Prescribe specific symptom-targeted medications according to professional level.
Monitor changes in symptoms.
Encourage the woman to participate in a support group.
Refer to a specialist, as appropriate.

definicao: Use of technological equipment and devices to monitor the patient's condition or sustain life.
atividades: Change or replace patient care equipment as per protocol.
Provide backup equipment, as appropriate.
Maintain equipment in good working order.
Correct defective equipment.
Zero and calibrate equipment, as appropriate.
Keep emergency equipment properly maintained and readily accessible.
Ensure proper grounding of electronic equipment.
Connect equipment to electrical outlets connected to an emergency power source.
Have equipment periodically checked by biomedical engineering, as appropriate.
Recharge batteries of portable patient care equipment.
Set alarm limits on equipment, as appropriate.
Respond appropriately to equipment alarms.
Consult with other healthcare team members and recommend equipment or devices for patient use.

definicao: Assisting the patient through the steps of complex infertility treatment.
atividades: Provide counseling on various treatment modalities (intrauterine insemination, in vitro fertilization-embryo transfer (IVF-ET), gamete intrafallopian transfer (GIFT), zygote intrafallopian transfer (ZIFT), donor sperm, donor oocytes, surrogacy).
Discuss ethical dilemmas before initiating a particular treatment modality.
Explore feelings about assisted reproduction technology (known versus anonymous oocyte or sperm donors, embryo cryopreservation, selective reduction, and use of a host uterus).
Refer for preconception counseling, as needed.
Instruct the patient on ovulation prediction and detection techniques (e.g., basal body temperature and urine tests).
Instruct the patient on the administration of ovulation stimulants.
Schedule tests, if needed, based on the menstrual cycle.
Coordinate activities of the multidisciplinary team for the treatment process.
Assist individuals living away to locate accommodation while participating in the program.
Provide counseling to gamete donors and their partners.
Collaborate with the IVF team in screening and selecting gamete donors.
Explore psychosocial issues involving gamete donation before administering comfort medications.
Coordinate synchronization between the donor and the recipient's hormonal cycle.
Collect specimens for endocrine determination.
Perform ultrasound examinations to assess follicular growth.
Participate in team meetings to correlate test results for oocyte maturity assessment.
Adjust equipment for oocyte retrieval.
Assist with embryo freezing and preservation, as indicated.
Assist with fertilization processes.
Prepare the patient for embryo transfer.
Provide anticipatory guidance on typical emotional reactions, including extremes of distress and joy.
Discuss risks, including the likelihood of miscarriage, ectopic pregnancy, and ovarian hyperstimulation.
Counsel on precautions against ectopic pregnancies.
Instruct on symptoms of ovarian hyperstimulation to report.
Perform pregnancy tests.
Provide support for periods of grief when implantation fails.
Schedule follow-up for medications, tests, and ultrasound examinations.
Assist in hormonal and ultrasound monitoring in early pregnancies.
Refer for genetic counseling when needed, related to maternal age at conception.
Refer to infertility support groups, as needed.
Follow up with patients who have discontinued treatment due to pregnancy, adoption, or decision not to have more children.
Assist patients in focusing their lives on areas of success unrelated to fertility status.
Instruct on methods of obtaining support in the workplace for necessary absences during treatment.
Provide guidance on financial matters and health insurance coverage.
Participate in the registration of data on treatment outcomes in national registries.

definicao: Assisting the patient in receiving artificial respiration support via equipment inserted into the trachea.
atividades: Monitor for conditions indicating the need for ventilatory support (e.g., respiratory muscle fatigue, neurological dysfunction secondary to trauma, anesthesia, substance intoxication, refractory respiratory acidosis).

definicao: Assisting a patient receiving artificial respiratory support that does not require a device inserted into the trachea.
atividades: Monitor for conditions indicating non-invasive ventilatory support (acute exacerbations of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease [COPD], asthma, non-cardiogenic and cardiogenic pulmonary edema, acute respiratory failure due to community-acquired pneumonia, obesity hypoventilation syndrome, obstructive sleep apnea).

Monitor for contraindications to non-invasive ventilatory support (hemodynamic instability, cardiovascular or respiratory arrest, unstable angina, acute myocardial infarction, refractory hypoxemia, severe respiratory acidosis, decreased level of consciousness, problems with positioning/placement of non-invasive equipment, facial trauma, inability to cooperate, morbid obesity, thick secretions, or bleeding).
Consult another healthcare professional to select a ventilator for the

definicao: Care of a patient at risk for developing ventilator-associated pneumonia.
atividades: Wash hands before and after patient care activity, especially after emptying ventilator circuit fluids.
Use gloves, protective equipment, and apparel for care and change gloves to prevent cross-contamination during oral hygiene.
Monitor the oral cavity, lips, tongue, buccal mucosa, and teeth condition.
Monitor the oral cavity for dental plaque, inflammation, hemorrhage, candidiasis, purulent matter, calculus, and staining.
Brush teeth and tongue with toothpaste or an antiseptic mouthwash, using circular motions with a soft brush or suction toothbrush.
Wash the toothbrush after each use and replace it at regular intervals.
Gently brush the gums if the patient is edentulous.
Assist in applying a debriding agent or mouthwash to gums, teeth, and tongue with a swab, according to institution protocol.
Use water rinses instead of a debriding agent in patients who have mucositis or altered oral mucosa.
Assist by cleaning perpendicular to the gum line while applying gentle pressure to help facilitate debris and mucus removal.
Consider povidone-iodine oral antiseptic in patients with severe head injury.
Consult the dentist, if needed.
Apply oral moisturizer to oral mucosa and lips, as needed.
Facilitate the use of a Yankauer or gentle suction for oral care, as needed.
Facilitate subglottic aspiration before repositioning the patient supine (bed, chair, road trip), repositioning the endotracheal tube (ET), and deflating the ET cuff.
Suction the trachea, then the oral cavity, and then the nasal pharynx to remove secretions above the ET cuff to decrease the risk of

definicao: Identification, treatment, and prevention of allergic reactions to food, medications, insect bites, contrast material, blood, and other substances.
atividades: Identify known allergies (e.g., medication, food, insects, environmental) and usual reactions.
Notify caregivers and healthcare providers about known allergies.
Document all allergies in the clinical record according to protocol.
Place an allergy identification bracelet on the patient, as appropriate.
Monitor the patient for allergic reactions to new medications, formulas, foods, latex, and/or test dyes.

Monitor the patient after exposures to agents known to cause allergic reactions, looking for signs of generalized flushing, angioedema, urticaria, paroxysmal cough, severe anxiety, dyspnea, sneezing, orthopnea, vomiting, cyanosis, or shock.
Keep the patient under observation for 30 minutes after administration of an agent known to be capable of inducing allergic reaction.
Instruct the patient with medication allergies to question all new prescriptions for possible allergic reactions.
Encourage the patient to use an allergy alert bracelet, as appropriate.
Immediately identify the level of threat an allergic reaction poses to the patient's health condition.
Monitor for recurrence of anaphylaxis within 24 hours.
Provide life support measures during anaphylactic shock or severe reactions.
Provide medications to reduce or minimize an allergic reaction.
Assist with allergy testing, as appropriate.
Administer allergy injections, if needed.
Observe for allergic reactions during immunizations.
Instruct patient/parents to avoid allergenic substances, as appropriate.
Instruct patient/parents on how to treat rashes, vomiting, diarrhea, or respiratory problems associated with exposure to allergenic substances.
Instruct the patient to avoid future use of substances causing allergic reactions.
Discuss methods of controlling environmental allergens (e.g., dust, mold, and pollen).
Instruct the patient and caregiver(s) on how to avoid situations that put them at risk and how to react if an anaphylactic reaction occurs.
Instruct the patient and caregiver on the use of an epinephrine pen.

definicao: Promoting safety, comfort, and reality orientation for a patient experiencing hallucinations.
atividades: Establish a trusting interpersonal relationship with the patient.
Monitor and regulate the level of activity and stimulation in the environment.
Maintain a safe environment.
Provide an appropriate level of surveillance/supervision to monitor the patient.
Record patient behaviors indicating hallucinations.
Maintain a consistent routine.
Ensure consistent caregivers in the daily routine.
Promote clear and open communication.
Use concrete statements rather than abstract statements when speaking with the patient.
Offer the patient opportunities to discuss the hallucinations.
Encourage the patient to express feelings appropriately.
Redirect the patient to the topic if their communication is inappropriate to the circumstances.
Monitor for the presence of violent or self-harmful content in the hallucinations.
Encourage the patient to develop control and responsibility over their own behavior, if their ability allows.
Encourage the patient to discuss feelings and impulses rather than act on them.
Encourage the patient to validate hallucinations with trusted people (e.g., reality testing).
State, if asked, that you are not experiencing the same stimulus.
Avoid arguing with the patient about the validity of the hallucinations.
Focus discussion on underlying feelings rather than the content of the hallucinations (e.g., 'It sounds like you're feeling scared').

definicao: Obtaining, preparing, and preserving a specimen for laboratory examination.
atividades: Obtain the requested specimen according to protocol.
Instruct the patient on how to collect and preserve the specimen, as appropriate.
Provide the container for the requested specimen.
Use special specimen collection devices as needed for infants, young children, or impaired adults.
Assist with tissue or organ biopsy, as appropriate.
Assist with aspiration of fluid from a body cavity, as appropriate.
Store the collected specimen over time according to protocol.
Seal all specimen containers to prevent leakage or contamination.
Label the specimen with appropriate data before leaving the patient.
Place the specimen in an appropriate transport container.
Arrange transport of the specimen to the laboratory.
Order routine laboratory tests related to the specimen, as appropriate.

definicao: Prevention, recognition, and facilitation of treatment of abnormal cardiac rhythms.
atividades: Assess patient and family history of cardiac disease and arrhythmias.
Monitor and correct oxygenation deficits, acid-base imbalances, and electrolyte imbalances that may precipitate arrhythmias.
Apply wireless electrocardiographic (ECG) telemetry or wired electrodes and connect to a cardiac monitor, as indicated.
Ensure proper lead selection for patient needs.
Ensure proper lead placement and signal quality.
Set alarm parameters on the ECG monitor.
Ensure continuous bedside ECG monitoring by qualified personnel.
Monitor ECG changes that increase the risk of developing arrhythmia (e.g., arrhythmia, ST segment, ischemia, and QT interval).
Facilitate obtaining a 12-lead ECG, as appropriate.
Observe activities associated with arrhythmia onset.
Observe the rate and duration of arrhythmia.
Monitor the hemodynamic response to arrhythmia.
Assess whether the patient has chest pain or syncope associated with the arrhythmia.
Ensure prompt access to emergency antiarrhythmic medications.
Initiate and maintain intravenous access, as appropriate.
Administer basic or advanced cardiac life support, if indicated.
Administer prescribed IV fluids and vasoconstrictor agents as indicated to facilitate tissue perfusion.
Assist with insertion of a temporary transvenous or external pacemaker, as appropriate.
Instruct the patient and family about the risks associated with arrhythmias.
Prepare the patient and family for diagnostic studies (e.g., cardiac catheterization or electrophysiological study).
Assist the patient and family in understanding treatment options.
Instruct the patient and family about actions and side effects of prescribed medications.
