Instruct the patient on workplace fundamentals (e.g., improving work performance, learning about specific workplace expectations and performance, making friends, and appropriate socialization).
Identify and organize participation in leisure activities.
Provide assistance in managing finances and creating a budget, if indicated.
Include family members or others as appropriate.

definicao: Facilitating excellent physical, cognitive, social, and emotional growth of individuals during the transition from childhood to adulthood.
atividades: Build a trusting relationship with the adolescent and their

definicao: Facilitating or teaching parents/caregivers for excellent fine and gross motor development, language, cognitive, social, and emotional development of children at preschool and school ages.
atividades: Build a trusting relationship with the child.
Establish an interaction between you and the child.
Help each child become aware that they are important as an individual.
Identify the child's special needs and necessary adaptations, as appropriate.
Build a trusting relationship with the caregivers.
Teach caregivers about normal developmental milestones and associated behaviors.
Demonstrate to caregivers activities that promote development.
Facilitate caregiver contact with community resources, as appropriate.
Refer caregivers to support groups, as appropriate.
Facilitate the child's integration with peers.
Ensure body language matches verbal communication.
Encourage the child to interact with others through role-playing in social interactions.
Provide activities that encourage children's interaction.
Help the child share and take turns.
Encourage the child to express themselves through positive rewards or feedback for attempts.
Hold or rock and comfort the child, especially when upset.
Reinforce cooperation and non-competitiveness among children.
Create a safe, well-defined space for the child to explore and learn.
Teach the child to seek help from others when needed.
Encourage daydreaming or fantasy when appropriate.
Offer age-appropriate toys or materials.
Help the child learn self-help skills (e.g., feeding, toileting, brushing teeth, washing hands, dressing).
Listen to music and discuss it.
Sing and talk with the child.
Encourage the child to sing and dance.
Teach the child to follow directions.
Facilitate role-playing of adults' daily activities in the child's world (e.g., pretend shopping, etc.).
Be consistent and organized with behavior management/modification strategies.
Redirect attention when needed.
Ask the misbehaving child to take a time-out to calm down.
Provide the opportunity and materials to build, draw, sculpt, paint, and color.
Assist with cutting and using glue in various shapes.
Provide opportunities to play with puzzles and mazes.
Teach the child to recognize and manipulate shapes.
Teach the child to write their name/recognize the first letter/recognize their name, as appropriate.
Name objects in the environment.
Tell or read stories to the child.
Work on sorting and sequencing letters, numbers, and objects.
Assist with spatial organization.
Teach planning by encouraging the child to imagine what will happen next and ask the child to make a list of other possible choices, and so on.
Provide opportunities and encourage exercise and gross motor activities.
Teach the child to jump over objects.
Teach the child to do somersaults.
Provide opportunities to play on the playground.
Take walks with the child.
Monitor the prescribed medication regimen, as appropriate.

definicao: Facilitating excellent physical, cognitive, social, and emotional development of children under 1 year of age.
atividades: Instruct parents on what constitutes adequate infant nutrition and feeding habits.
Discuss and support the decision to breastfeed or bottle-feed.
Instruct on proper storage, preparation, and handling of breast milk or infant formula.
Introduce solid foods at approximately 6 months of age, instructing parents on food selection and preparation, methods of introduction, and food storage.
Instruct parents to avoid giving the infant a bottle containing juice or milk while lying down.
Offer anticipatory guidance for weaning the infant, including signs of readiness.
Encourage establishing bedtime rituals that reduce or eliminate sleep-wake cycle disturbances.
Assist parents in identifying the presence of sleep imbalance or disorder.
Determine the appropriate control technique for the sleep imbalance or disorder.
Discuss the risks and benefits of co-sleeping with the infant.
Provide visual, auditory, tactile, and kinesthetic stimulation during play.
Structure play and care according to the infant's behavioral style and temperament patterns.
Provide safe, developmentally appropriate toys and activities.
Explain the need for fluoride supplementation, starting at 6 months of age.
Instruct parents to begin cleaning the infant's oral cavity with a damp cloth after the first tooth erupts.
Use a soft-bristled toothbrush with water or non-fluoride toothpaste once several teeth have erupted.
Determine the appropriate schedule for initial and subsequent dental check-ups.
Perform recommended screenings (e.g., anemia, lead exposure, and vision).
Provide accurate information regarding risks, benefits, contraindications, and side effects of scheduled immunizations.
Identify the need for additional immunizations for selected groups of children.
Provide anticipatory guidance regarding discipline, dependence, increased mobility, and safety.
Discuss time-out versus corporal punishment strategies for discipline, encouraging the former.
Instruct parents on injury prevention strategies according to the child's specific developmental stage and level of curiosity.
Encourage providing a safe space for the infant to explore.
Discuss strategies for preventing burns from fire and electricity, suffocation and aspiration, poisoning, falls, bodily injury, drowning, and vehicle trauma.
Instruct parents on strategies for preventing sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS).
Instruct parents on the child's temperament and its association with the infant's interaction style with others.
Promote and facilitate family bonding and attachment with the infant.
Support and praise parents' skills and efforts.
Discuss parents' return to work and child care options.
Identify and address the presence of family conflict, lack of support, and pathology.
Provide parents with information on child development and parenting.
Refer for parenting education, as needed.

definicao: Facilitating cognitive and behavioral efforts to manage stressors, changes, or perceived threats that interfere with meeting life demands and roles.
atividades: Assist the patient in identifying appropriate short- and long-term goals.
Assist the patient in examining available resources to achieve goals.
Assist the patient in breaking complex goals into small, manageable steps.
Encourage relationships with people who have common interests and goals.
Assist the patient in solving problems constructively.
Assess the patient's adjustment to body image changes, as indicated.
Assess the impact of the patient's life situation on roles and relationships.
Encourage the patient to identify a realistic description of role change.
Assess the patient's understanding of the disease process.
Assess and discuss alternative responses to the situation.
Use a calm, reassuring approach.
Provide an environment of acceptance.
Assist the patient in developing an objective evaluation of the event.
Assist the patient in identifying the information they are most interested in obtaining.
Provide factual information regarding diagnosis, treatment, and prognosis.
Provide the patient with realistic options about certain aspects of care.
Encourage an attitude of realistic hope as a way of coping with feelings of helplessness.
Assess the patient's ability to make decisions.
Seek to understand the patient's perspective on a stressful situation.
Discourage decision-making when the patient is under severe stress.

definicao: Assisting people with limited ability to obtain, process, and understand health and illness-related information.
atividades: Create a healthcare environment where a patient with low educational level can seek help without feeling ashamed or stigmatized.
Use clear and appropriate communication.
Use plain language.
Simplify language whenever possible.
Use a slow speaking pace.
Avoid medical jargon and use of abbreviations.
Communicate considering cultural, age, and gender appropriateness.
Determine the patient's experiences with the healthcare system, including health promotion and protection, disease prevention, medical care, and maintenance and use of the healthcare system.
Determine health literacy status at initial patient contact through formal and/or informal assessment.
Determine the patient's learning style.

Observe for signs of impaired health literacy (e.g., inability to complete a form, missing appointments, not taking medications properly, inability to identify medications or describe reasons for taking them, deferring information about health status to family members, asking multiple questions about topics already covered in handouts and pamphlets, avoiding reading in front of healthcare professionals).
Obtain information services as needed.
Provide basic written and oral information to the patient in their native language.
Determine what the patient already knows about their health condition or risks, and relate new information to what is already known.
Provide individual education and counseling whenever possible.

Provide understandable written materials (e.g., short sentences and common words with few syllables, key points highlighted, active voice, large print, user-friendly format and design, similar content grouped into segments, emphasis on behaviors and actions to be taken, use of pictures or diagrams to simplify, and reduction of heavy reading).

Use strategies to improve comprehension (e.g., present the most important information first, focus on key messages and repetition, limit the amount of information presented at one time, use examples to illustrate key points, relate to individual experiences, use storytelling).
Use various communication tools (e.g., audio tapes, video tapes, digital video devices, computers, pictograms, models, diagrams).
Assess patient understanding by having them repeat in their own words or demonstrate the skill.
Encourage the individual to ask questions and seek clarification (e.g., What is my main problem? What do I need to do? Why is it important for me to do this?).

Help the person anticipate their experiences in the healthcare system (e.g., being asked questions, seeing different healthcare professionals, keeping care informed when information is not understood, collecting laboratory results, scheduling and keeping appointments).

Encourage the use of effective strategies to cope with health literacy deficits (e.g., being persistent when asking for help, bringing a list of questions or concerns in writing to each healthcare encounter, relying on oral explanations or demonstrations of tasks, seeking assistance from family or friends in seeking health information).

definicao: Assisting a patient, friends, and/or family members to improve relationships by clarifying and supplementing specific behaviors.
atividades: Assist the patient in identifying the various roles in the life cycle.
Assist the patient in identifying their usual role in the family.

definicao: Facilitating support for the patient from family, friends, and community.
atividades: Identify the psychological response to the situation and the availability of the support system.
Determine the adequacy of existing social networks.
Identify the degree of family support, financial support, and other resources.
Determine barriers to unused and underutilized support systems.
Monitor the current family situation and support network.
Encourage the patient to participate in social and community activities.
Encourage relationships with people who have common interests and goals.
Refer to a self-help group or internet-based resource, as appropriate.
Identify strengths and weaknesses of community resources and advocate for changes, as appropriate.
Refer to a community treatment or prevention program, as appropriate.
Provide services in a caring and supportive manner.
Involve the family, significant other(s), and friends in care and planning.
Identify available resources to support the caregiver.
Explain to other interested parties how they can help.

definicao: Facilitating regular sleep/wake cycles.
atividades: Determine the patient's sleep/activity pattern.
Approximate the patient's sleep/wake cycle when planning care.
Explain the importance of adequate sleep during pregnancy, illness, psychological stress, etc.
Determine the effects of the patient's medications on sleep patterns.
Monitor/record the patient's sleep pattern and number of hours slept.

Monitor the patient's sleep pattern and observe physical circumstances (e.g., sleep apnea, airway obstruction, pain/discomfort, and urinary frequency) and/or psychological circumstances (e.g., fear or anxiety) that disrupt sleep.
Instruct the patient to monitor sleep patterns.
Monitor participation in fatigue-generating activities during wakefulness to prevent excessive tiredness.
Adjust the environment (e.g., light, noise, temperature, mattress, and bed) to promote sleep.
Encourage the patient to establish a bedtime routine to facilitate the transition from wakefulness to sleep.

Facilitate maintenance of the patient's usual bedtime routines, pre-sleep cues/support, and familiar objects (e.g., for children, a favorite blanket/toy, rocking, pacifier, or stories; for adults, a book, etc.), as appropriate.
Assist in eliminating stressful situations before bedtime.
Monitor food and fluid intake at bedtime, considering items that facilitate or interfere with sleep.
Instruct the patient to avoid foods and beverages that interfere with sleep.
Assist the patient in limiting daytime sleep by engaging in activities that promote wakefulness, when appropriate.
Instruct the patient to perform autogenic muscle relaxation or other non-pharmacological sleep induction methods.
Initiate/implement comfort measures such as massage, positioning, and affective touch.
Promote an increase in the number of hours of sleep as needed.
Provide daytime naps if indicated to meet sleep needs.
Group therapeutic activities to minimize the number of awakenings; provide at least 90-minute sleep cycles.
Adjust the medication administration schedule to support the patient's sleep/wake cycle.

Instruct the patient and other significant others about factors (e.g., physiological, psychological, lifestyle, frequent shift changes, rapid time zone changes, excessively long working hours, and other environmental factors) that contribute to sleep pattern disturbances.
Identify which sleep medications the patient is taking.

definicao: Facilitating skin-to-skin contact between parents or other caregiver and the physiologically stable preterm infant.
atividades: Explain the advantages and implications of providing skin-to-skin contact to the infant.
Monitor influencing factors for the mother regarding care (willingness, health, availability, and presence of support system).
Ensure the infant's physiological status meets the guidelines for participation in care.
Prepare a quiet, private, and warm environment.
Offer parents a reclining chair or rocking chair.
Instruct the mother to wear comfortable, front-opening clothing.
Instruct parents on how to transfer the infant from the incubator, radiant warmer, or crib while managing equipment and tubes.
Position a diaper lining on the newborn's chest, vertically or on the mother's chest.
Turn the infant's head to one side, in a slightly extended position, to facilitate eye contact with the mother and maintain a clear airway.
Avoid flexing forward and hyperextending the infant's head.
The infant's hips and arms should be flexed.
Position the infant according to the mother's/father's position (i.e., tie a cloth (sling) around the infant-mother, wrap/swaddle a wrap around the mother and the infant).
Instruct mother/father on how to move the infant in and out of the sling.
Encourage mother/father to focus on the infant, not the environment and high-technology equipment.
Encourage mother/father to gently stroke the infant in the prone upright position.
Encourage mother/father to gently rock the infant in the prone upright position.
Encourage auditory stimulation of the infant.
Support the mother in nurturing and providing contact in infant care.
Instruct mother/father to hold the infant completely, encompassing them with their hands.
Encourage mother/father to identify the infant's behavioral cues.
Highlight the infant's physiological changes to the parents.
Encourage mother/father to sit, stand, walk, and engage in other activities of interest while maintaining skin-to-skin contact.
Encourage postpartum mothers to walk every 90 minutes while providing skin-to-skin contact to prevent thromboembolic disease.
Instruct the mother to decrease activity when the infant shows signs of hyperstimulation, discomfort, or avoidance.
Encourage the mother to let the infant sleep during care.
Encourage breastfeeding during care when appropriate.
Encourage the mother to provide care for at least 60 minutes, to avoid frequent and potentially stressful changes, if possible.
Instruct mother/father to gradually increase the time of each skin-to-skin contact, eventually making the duration as continuous as possible.
Monitor the parents' emotional reaction and concerns regarding the kangaroo method.
Monitor the infant's physiological status (color, temperature, heart rate, and apnea).
Instruct parents on how to monitor the infant's physiological status.
Support parents in continuing skin-to-skin contact at home.
Discontinue care if the infant becomes physiologically compromised or agitated.

definicao: Promoting urinary continence through the use of verbal reminders at scheduled times for toileting and positive social feedback for successful toileting.
atividades: Determine the ability to recognize urinary urgency.
Maintain a 3-day continence record to establish a voiding pattern.
Use for patients who do not exhibit signs and symptoms of overflow and/or reflex urinary incontinence.
Establish the initial interval of the scheduled toileting schedule based on the voiding pattern.
Establish the start and end time of the scheduled toileting schedule, even if for 24 hours.
Approach 15 minutes before the scheduled toileting time.
Allow time (5 seconds) for the patient to ask for help to use the toilet.
Determine the patient's awareness of continence status by asking if they are wet or dry.
Determine the accuracy of the response by physically checking clothing or bed linens, as appropriate.
Provide positive feedback on the accuracy of the response to continence status and success in maintaining continence between scheduled toileting times.
Determine the use of the toilet or substitute (maximum of three times) regardless of continence status.
Assist during the use of the toilet, regardless of continence status.
Provide privacy for toileting.
Give positive feedback by praising a desired toileting behavior.
Do not comment on incontinence or refusal to use the toilet.
Inform the patient of the time of the next toileting session.
Instruct the patient to consciously control urination between toileting sessions in cases where no cognitive impairment exists.
Instruct the patient to voluntarily use the toilet in response to urinary urgency.
Document the results of the toileting session in the medical record.
Discuss a continence record with the nursing staff to generate reinforcing attitudes and encourage adherence to a schedule of

definicao: Utilization of family strengths to positively influence the patient's health.
atividades: Be a listener for family members.
Establish trusting relationships with family members.
View family members as potential experts in patient care.
Identify strengths and resources in the family, family members, and their support and community systems.
Determine the readiness and ability of family members to learn.
Provide frequent information to the family to help them identify patient limitations, progress, and implications for patient care.
Promote mutual decision-making with family members regarding the patient's care plan.
Teach home caregivers about the patient's therapy, as appropriate.
Explain to family members the need to maintain professional healthcare, as appropriate.
Collaborate with family members in planning and implementing the patient's therapies and lifestyle changes.
Support family activities that promote patient health or health status management, when appropriate.
Support family members in identifying health services and community resources that can be used to improve the patient's health status.
Monitor the current family situation.
Refer family members to support groups, as appropriate.
Systematically determine the achievement of expected patient outcomes.

definicao: Promoting behavior change.
atividades: Determine the patient's motivation to change.
Assist the patient in identifying strengths and reinforce them.
Encourage substitution of undesirable habits with desirable ones.
Introduce the patient to people (or groups) who have successfully faced the same experience.
Ensure the intervention is implemented consistently by the entire team.
Reinforce constructive decisions about health needs.
Offer feedback in terms of feelings when observing that the patient has no symptoms and appears relaxed.
Avoid showing rejection or disdain when the patient is struggling to change their behavior.
Offer positive reinforcement for decisions made independently by the patient.
Encourage the patient to examine their own behavior.
Assist the patient in identifying even small successes.
Identify the patient's problem in behavioral terms.
Identify the behavior to be changed (target behavior) in specific, concrete terms.
Break the behavior to be changed into smaller, measurable units of behavior (e.g., stopping smoking: number of cigarettes smoked).
Use specific time periods when measuring behavior units (e.g., number of cigarettes smoked per day).
Determine whether the identified target behavior needs to be increased, decreased, or learned.
Consider that it is easier to increase a behavior than to decrease it.
Establish behavioral goals in writing.
Develop a behavior change program.
Establish a baseline occurrence of the behavior before initiating change.
Develop a method (e.g., figure or table) to record the behavior and its modifications.
Encourage the patient to participate in monitoring and recording behaviors.
Discuss the behavior modification process with the patient and significant others.
Facilitate the involvement of other healthcare providers in the modification process, as appropriate.
Facilitate the involvement of the family in the modification process, as appropriate.
Provide positive reinforcement for desired behaviors at predetermined times (continuous or intermittent).
Withdraw positive reinforcement for undesired behaviors and add reinforcement for a more desirable substitute behavior.
Encourage the patient to participate in selecting meaningful reinforcers.
Choose reinforcers that can be controlled (e.g., used only when the behavior to be changed occurs).
Coordinate a token or point reinforcement system for complex or multiple behaviors.
Develop a treatment contract with the patient to support the implementation of the token/point system.
Promote skill acquisition by systematically reinforcing simple components of the skill or task.
Promote learning of the desired behavior using modeling techniques.
Determine behavior changes by comparing baseline occurrences with post-intervention behavior occurrences.
Document and communicate the modification process to the treatment team as needed.
Follow up with long-term reinforcement (phone or in-person contact).

definicao: Assisting the patient in developing or improving interpersonal social skills.
atividades: Assist the patient in identifying interpersonal problems resulting from social skills deficits.
Consider using a behavioral training program of

definicao: Collection and analysis of patient data to regulate acid-base balance.
atividades: Obtain requested samples for laboratory analysis of acid-base balance (e.g., blood for arterial blood gases, urine, and serum) in at-risk populations, as appropriate.
Obtain sequential samples to check trends.
Analyze trends in serum pH in patients with conditions that have upward effects on pH levels (e.g., hyperventilating patients, patients with diabetic or alcoholic ketoacidosis, septic patients).
Analyze trends in serum pH in at-risk populations (e.g., patients with compromised respiratory status, renal impairment, diabetes mellitus, prolonged diarrhea or vomiting, Cushing's syndrome).
Observe whether the arterial pH level is on the alkaline or acid side of the mean (7.35 to 7.45).
Observe whether the PaCO2 level shows respiratory acidosis, respiratory alkalosis, or normalcy.
Observe whether the HCO3 levels show metabolic acidosis, metabolic alkalosis, or normalcy.
Examine trends in serum pH in conjunction with trends in PaCO2 and HCO3 to verify whether acidosis or alkalosis is compensated or not.
Observe whether compensation is pulmonary, metabolic, or physiologically buffered.
Identify possible etiologies before attempting to treat acid-base imbalances, as it is more effective to treat the etiology than the imbalance.
Identify the presence or absence of anion gap (greater than 14 mEq/L), indicating increased production or decreased excretion of acid products.

Monitor signs and symptoms of HCO3 deficit and metabolic acidosis (e.g., Kussmaul-Kien breathing, weakness, disorientation, headache, anorexia, coma, urine pH less than 6, plasma HCO3 less than 22 mEq/L, plasma pH less than 7.35, base excess less than -2 mEq/L, associated hyperkalemia, and possible CO2 deficit).

Monitor causes of metabolic acidosis (e.g., methanol or ethanol ingestion, uremia, diabetic ketoacidosis, alcoholic ketoacidosis, para-aldehyde ingestion, lactic acidosis, sepsis, hypotension, hypoxia, ischemia, malnutrition, diarrhea, renal failure, hyperalimentation, hyperparathyroidism, salicylate toxicity, ethylene glycol ingestion).

Monitor signs and symptoms of HCO3 excess and metabolic alkalosis (e.g., numbness and tingling of extremities, muscle hypertonicity, shallow breathing with pauses, bradycardia, tetany, urine pH greater than 7, plasma HCO3 greater than 26 mEq/L, plasma pH greater than 7.45, base excess greater than 2 mEq/L, associated hypokalemia, and possible CO2 retention).

Monitor causes of metabolic alkalosis (e.g., diuretics, emesis, nasogastric tube, post-hypercapnia, potassium deficiency, base ingestion, Cushing's syndrome, hyperaldosteronism, hypochloremia, excessive intake of HCO3-containing medications).

Monitor signs and symptoms of PaCO2 deficit and respiratory alkalosis (e.g., frequent sighing and yawning, tetany, paresthesias, muscle spasms, palpitations, tingling and numbness, dizziness, blurred vision, diaphoresis, dry mouth, seizures, pH greater than 7.45, PaCO2 less than 35 mmHg, associated hyperchloremia, and possible HCO3 deficit).
Monitor causes of respiratory alkalosis (e.g., hyperventilation, mechanical hyperventilation, liver disease, pregnancy, septicemia, pain, CNS lesions, and fever).

Monitor signs and symptoms of PaCO2 excess and respiratory acidosis (e.g., hand tremor extending to arms, confusion, drowsiness progressing to coma, headache, decreased verbal response, nausea, vomiting, tachycardia, warm and moist extremities, pH less than 7.35, PaCO2 greater than 45 mmHg, associated hypochloremia, and possible HCO3 excess).

Monitor possible causes of respiratory acidosis (e.g., airway obstruction, ventilatory depression, CNS depression, neurological diseases, chronic lung disease, musculoskeletal diseases, chest trauma, infection, ARDS, heart failure, acute opioid ingestion, and use of respiratory depressant medications).
Compare current status with previous status to detect improvement and deterioration in the patient's condition.
Initiate and/or change treatment to maintain patient parameters within physician-requested limits using established protocols.

definicao: Measurement and interpretation of data to regulate intracranial pressure.
atividades: Assist with insertion of ICP monitoring equipment.
Provide information to the patient and family/significant others.
Calibrate the transducer.
Level the external transducer to a consistent anatomical reference point.
Flush and prime the system appropriately.
Set monitor alarms.
Record ICP pressure readings.
Monitor the quality and characteristics of the ICP waveform.
Monitor cerebral perfusion pressure.
Monitor neurological status.
Monitor the patient's ICP and neurological response to care activities and environmental stimuli.
Monitor the amount, rate, and characteristics of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) drainage.
Maintain the CSF collection chamber at the prescribed position.
Monitor fluid intake and output.
Prevent displacement of equipment.
Maintain sterility of the monitoring system.
Monitor pressure tubing for air bubbles, debris, or clotted blood.
Change the transducer, flush the system, and drainage bag as indicated.
Change and/or reinforce insertion site dressings when needed.
Monitor the insertion site for infection or fluid leakage.
Obtain CSF samples when appropriate.
Monitor temperature and white blood cell count.
Check for nuchal rigidity.
Administer antibiotics.
Position the patient with head and neck in a neutral position, avoiding extreme hip flexion.
Adjust the head of the bed to optimize cerebral perfusion.
Monitor the effect of environmental stimuli on ICP.
Space nursing care to minimize ICP elevation.
Modify the suctioning procedure to minimize ICP elevation with catheter insertion (e.g., administer lidocaine and limit the number of suction catheter passes).
Monitor CO2 levels and maintain within appropriate parameters.
Maintain systemic blood pressure within the specified range.
Administer pharmacological agents to maintain ICP within the specified range.
Notify the physician for ICP elevation that does not respond to treatment protocols.

definicao: Collection, analysis, and use of patient data to categorize risk and prevent lower extremity injury.
atividades: Inspect the skin for evidence of poor hygiene.
Inspect the lower extremities for the presence of edema.
Inspect for changes in toenails (e.g., thickening, fungal infection, ingrown nails, and evidence of improper trimming).
Inspect the skin for color, temperature, hydration, hair growth, texture, or fissures.
Inspect between the toes for maceration, cracks, or fissures.
Inquire about changes in the feet and current or past history of ulcers or amputation.
Determine mobility status (i.e., walks without assistance, walks with assistance of a gait aid, or does not walk, uses a wheelchair).
Inspect the foot for deformities, including hammertoes, prominent metatarsal heads, and high or low arches, or Charcot changes.
Monitor ankle and foot muscle strength.
Inspect the foot for evidence of pressure (i.e., presence of localized redness, increased temperature, blisters, callus formation).
Inquire about the presence of paresthesias (e.g., numbness, tingling, or burning).
Palpate the thickness of fat pads over the metatarsal heads.
Palpate the dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses.
Determine the ankle-brachial index, as indicated.
Inquire about the presence of intermittent claudication, rest pain, or nocturnal pain.
Determine capillary refill time.
Monitor the level of protective sensation using the Semmes-Weinstein nylon monofilament.
Determine the vibration perception threshold.
Determine proprioceptive responses.
Elicit deep tendon reflexes (i.e., ankle and knee), as indicated.
Monitor gait and weight distribution on the feet (e.g., observe walking and determine the shoe wear pattern).
Monitor the condition of shoes and socks (i.e., clean and in good condition).

Monitor the adequacy of shoes (e.g., low heel, a shape matching the foot shape; adequate depth for toes; soles made of shock-absorbing material; fit by laces or straps; upper made of breathable, soft, flexible material; modifications made for gait and limb length disturbances, and potential for modification, if needed).
Monitor the adequacy of socks (i.e., absorbent material and non-constricting).
Monitor joint mobility (e.g., ankle dorsiflexion and subtalar joint movement).
Perform ongoing surveillance, at least four times a year, of the lower extremities to determine the need for referral.
Use the level of injury risk as a guide to determine appropriate referral.

Identify specialized foot care services (e.g., orthopedic or prescription footwear, need for callus trimming, need for nail trimming, mobility assessment and exercises, foot deformity assessment and management, treatment of skin/nail deformities/infection, correction of abnormal gait or weight-bearing, and/or assessment and management of impaired arterial circulation).
Consult the physician about recommendations for further assessments and therapies (e.g., X-rays), as needed.

definicao: Collection and analysis of patient data to regulate electrolyte balance.
atividades: Monitor serum electrolyte levels.
Monitor serum albumin and total protein levels, as indicated.
Monitor associated acid-base imbalances.
Identify possible causes of electrolyte imbalances.
Recognize and communicate the presence of electrolyte imbalances.
Monitor fluid loss and associated electrolyte loss, as appropriate.
Monitor for Chvostek's and/or Trousseau's signs.
Monitor neurological manifestations of electrolyte imbalance (e.g., sensory changes and weakness).
Monitor the adequacy of ventilation.
Monitor serum and urine osmolality levels.
Monitor changes related to abnormal potassium, calcium, and magnesium levels on ECG tracings.
Observe changes such as paresthesia and tremors in extremities.
Observe muscle strength.
Monitor nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea.
Identify treatments that may alter electrolyte status, such as gastrointestinal aspiration, diuretics, antihypertensives, and calcium channel blockers.
Monitor underlying disease that may lead to electrolyte imbalance.

Monitor signs and symptoms of hypokalemia: muscle weakness; cardiac irregularities (premature ventricular contractions [PVCs]); prolonged QT interval; flat or depressed T wave; ST segment depression; presence of U wave; fatigue; paresthesias; hypoactive reflexes; anorexia; constipation; decreased gastrointestinal motility; dizziness; confusion; increased sensitivity to digitalis; respiratory depression.

Monitor signs/symptoms of hyperkalemia: irritability, agitation; anxiety, nausea; vomiting; abdominal cramps, weakness; flaccid paralysis, perioral numbness and tingling; tachycardia progressing to bradycardia; ventricular tachycardia/fibrillation; peaked T waves; flat P wave; widened QRS complex; and heart block progressing to asystole.
Monitor signs/symptoms of hyponatremia: disorientation, muscle spasms, nausea and vomiting, abdominal cramps, headaches, personality changes, seizures, lethargy, fatigue, withdrawal, and coma.
Monitor signs and symptoms of hypernatremia: intense thirst; fever, dry and sticky mucous membranes, tachycardia, hypotension, lethargy, confusion, altered mental status; and seizure.

Monitor signs and symptoms of hypocalcemia: irritability; muscle tetany; Chvostek's sign (facial muscle spasm); Trousseau's sign (carpal spasm); paresthesia and numbness of extremities; muscle cramps; decreased cardiac output; ST segment and QT interval prolongation; bleeding; and fractures.
Monitor signs and symptoms of hypercalcemia: deep bone pain; excessive thirst; anorexia; lethargy; weak muscles; shortened QT segment; peaked T wave; widened QRS complex; and prolonged P-R interval.

Monitor signs and symptoms of hypomagnesemia: depression of respiratory muscles; mental apathy, Chvostek's sign (facial muscle spasm); Trousseau's sign (carpal spasm); confusion, facial tics; spasticity and cardiac arrhythmias.
Monitor signs and symptoms of hypermagnesemia: muscle weakness; inability to swallow; hyporeflexia; hypotension; bradycardia; CNS depression; respiratory depression; lethargy; coma; and depression.

Monitor signs and symptoms of hypophosphatemia: bleeding tendencies; muscle weakness; paresthesias; hemolytic anemia; decreased leukocyte function; nausea; vomiting; anorexia and bone demineralization.
Monitor signs and symptoms of hyperphosphatemia: tachycardia; nausea; diarrhea; abdominal cramps; muscle weakness; flaccid paralysis and hyperreflexia.
Monitor signs and symptoms of hypochloremia: hyperirritability; tetany; muscle excitability; slow respiration; and hypotension.
Monitor signs and symptoms of hyperchloremia: weakness; lethargy; rapid deep breathing; and coma.
Administer prescribed electrolyte supplements, as appropriate.
Provide the appropriate diet for the patient's electrolyte imbalance (e.g., potassium-rich foods or a low-sodium diet).
Teach the patient methods to prevent or minimize electrolyte imbalances.
Instruct the patient and family about specific dietary modifications, as appropriate.
Consult the physician if signs and symptoms of fluid and/or electrolyte imbalance persist or worsen.

definicao: Surveillance and influence of government and organizational regulation, rules, and standards that affect the healthcare system and nursing practice to ensure quality of patient care.
atividades: Review proposed policies and standards in organizational, professional, and governmental literature and in the popular media.
Monitor relevant websites (e.g., Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, state government policy sites) for ongoing policy changes that may affect patient care.
Compare requirements of new policies and standards with current practices.
Determine negative and positive effects of health policies.

definicao: Collection and analysis of cardiovascular, respiratory, and body temperature data to determine and prevent complications.
atividades: Monitor blood pressure, pulse, temperature, and respiratory status, as appropriate.
Observe trends and wide fluctuations in blood pressure.
Monitor blood pressure with the patient lying, sitting, and standing before and after position changes, as appropriate.
Monitor blood pressure after medication ingestion, if possible.
Measure blood pressures in both arms and compare, as appropriate.
Monitor blood pressure, pulse, and respirations before, during, and after activities, as appropriate.
Initiate and maintain continuous temperature monitoring, as appropriate.
Monitor and report signs and symptoms of hypothermia and hyperthermia.
Monitor the presence and quality of pulses.
Check apical and radial pulses simultaneously and observe for a difference, as appropriate.
Monitor for paradoxical pulse.
Monitor for pulsus alternans.
Monitor for increased or decreased pulse pressure.
Monitor cardiac rhythm and rate.
Monitor heart sounds.
Monitor respiratory rate and rhythm (e.g., depth and symmetry).
Monitor lung sounds.
Monitor pulse oximetry.
Monitor abnormal respiratory sounds (e.g., Cheyne-Stokes, Kussmaul, Biot, apnea, ataxia, excessive sighing).
Monitor skin color, temperature, and moisture.
Monitor for central and peripheral cyanosis.
Monitor for digital clubbing.
Monitor for Cushing's triad (e.g., wide pulse pressures, bradycardia, and increased systolic blood pressure).
Identify possible causes of changes in vital signs.
Periodically check the accuracy of instruments used for patient data acquisition.

definicao: Collection and analysis of patient data to regulate fluid balance.
atividades: Establish history of amount and type of fluid intake and elimination habits.

Determine possible risk factors for fluid volume imbalance (e.g., albumin loss status, burns, malnutrition, sepsis, nephrotic syndrome, hyperthermia, diuretic therapy, renal disease, heart failure, diaphoresis, hepatic dysfunction, vigorous exercise, heat exposure, infection, postoperative status, polyuria, vomiting, and diarrhea).
Determine if the patient is experiencing thirst or symptoms of fluid changes (e.g., dizziness, altered mental status, apprehension, irritability, nausea, muscle contractions).

Examine capillary refill by holding the patient's hand at heart level and pressing the nail bed of the middle finger for 5 seconds, releasing the pressure and counting the time until color returns (should be less than 2 seconds).

Examine skin turgor by gently pinching the tissue over a bony area, such as the hand or anterior leg, holding the position for one second and releasing (the skin will return quickly if the patient is well-hydrated).
Monitor weight.
Monitor intake and output.
Monitor serum and urine electrolyte values, as appropriate.
Monitor serum albumin and total protein levels.
Monitor serum and urine osmolality levels.
Monitor blood pressure, heart rate, and respiratory status.
Monitor orthostatic blood pressure and heart rate change, as appropriate.
Monitor invasive hemodynamic parameters, as appropriate.

Maintain an accurate intake and output record (e.g., oral intake, enteral administration, IV input, antibiotics, fluids given with medications, nasogastric (NG) tubes, drains, vomitus, rectal tubes, colostomy drainage, and urine output).

Measure all inputs and outputs in all patients with intravenous (IV) therapy, subcutaneous infusions, enteral feeding, NG tubes, urinary catheters, vomiting, diarrhea, wound drainage, chest tubes, and medical conditions affecting fluid volume balance (e.g., heart failure, renal failure, malnutrition, burns, sepsis).
Record incontinence episodes in patients requiring accurate fluid balance.
Correct mechanical problems (e.g., kinked or obstructed catheter) in patients with sudden cessation of urine output.
Monitor mucous membranes, skin turgor, and thirst.

definicao: Electronic assessment of fetal heart rate response to uterine contractions during intrapartum care.
atividades: Verify maternal and fetal heart rates before initiating electronic fetal monitoring.
Instruct the laboring woman and support person on the reason for electronic monitoring, as well as the information to be obtained.
Perform Leopold's maneuvers to determine fetal position.
Apply the tocotransducer gently to the fundus to observe the frequency, intensity, and duration of contractions.
Apply the ultrasound transducer to the area of the uterus where fetal heart tones are audible and the tracing is clear.
Differentiate multiple fetuses by documenting on the tracing when different simultaneous tracings are obtained using an electronic fetal monitor (e.g., baby A, baby B).
Distinguish multiple fetuses by comparing data when simultaneous tracings are obtained using two different fetal monitors.
Discuss aspects of the rhythm tracing with the mother and support person.
Reassure about normal fetal heart rate, including typical characteristics such as artifacts, signal loss due to fetal movement, high frequency, and irregular appearance.
Adjust monitors to obtain and maintain tracing clarity.
Evaluate the strip every 30 minutes in the first stage and every 15 minutes during the second stage.
Document elements of the external tracing (e.g., baseline fetal heart rate, oscillatory patterns, long-term variability, accelerations, decelerations, frequency, and duration of contractions).
Document relevant intrapartum care (e.g., vaginal exams, medication administration, maternal vital signs) directly on the monitor strip, as appropriate.
Remove electronic monitors before ambulation after verifying that the tracing is normal.
Use intermittent or telemetry fetal monitoring, if available, to facilitate maternal ambulation and comfort.
Initiate fetal resuscitation interventions to address concerning (abnormal) fetal heart rate patterns, as appropriate.
Document changes in fetal heart rate pattern after resuscitation.
Calibrate equipment, as appropriate, for internal monitoring with a spiral electrode and/or intrauterine pressure catheter.
Use universal precautions.
Apply the internal fetal electrode after membrane rupture to reduce artifacts or for assessment of short-term variability, when needed.
Apply the internal uterine pressure catheter after membrane rupture to obtain pressure data for uterine contractions and resting tone, when needed.
Document the maternal response to internal monitor application, including the degree of discomfort or pain, the appearance of amniotic fluid, and the presence of bleeding.
Document the fetal response to internal monitor placement, including short-term variability and accelerations or decelerations of fetal heart rate.
Keep the physician informed of pertinent changes in fetal heart rate, interventions for concerning patterns, subsequent fetal response, labor progress, and maternal response to labor.
Continue electronic monitoring during the second stage of labor or until cesarean delivery.
Remove internal monitors before cesarean delivery to prevent maternal infection.
Document monitor interpretation according to institutional policy.
Provide for the storage of the intrapartum strip as part of the patient's permanent record.

definicao: Electronic assessment of fetal heart rate response to movement, external stimuli, or uterine contractions during antepartum testing.
atividades: Review obstetric history, if possible, to determine obstetric or clinical risk factors requiring antepartum fetal status testing.
Determine the patient's knowledge of the reasons for antepartum testing.
Monitor maternal vital signs.

Provide the patient and family with written educational material about antepartum testing (e.g., non-stress, oxytocin challenge, biophysical profile tests), as well as about the electronic fetal monitor.
Inquire about oral intake, including diet, smoking, and medication use.
Label the monitor strip according to protocol.
Review previous antepartum tests.
Verify maternal and fetal heart rates before initiating electronic fetal monitoring.
Instruct the patient on the reason for electronic monitoring, as well as the types of information that can be obtained.
Encourage the mother to empty her bladder.
Perform Leopold's maneuvers to determine fetal position.

definicao: Measurement and interpretation of invasive hemodynamic parameters to determine cardiovascular function and appropriately regulate therapy.
atividades: Assist with insertion and removal of invasive hemodynamic catheters.
Assist with Allen's test for assessment of ulnar collateral circulation before radial artery cannulation, if appropriate.
Assist with chest radiograph after pulmonary artery catheter insertion.
Monitor heart rate and rhythm.
Zero and calibrate equipment every 4 to 12 hours, as appropriate, with the transducer at the right atrial level.

Monitor blood pressure (systolic, diastolic, and mean), central venous/right atrial pressure, pulmonary artery pressure (systolic, diastolic, and mean), and pulmonary capillary/pulmonary artery wedge pressure.
Monitor hemodynamic waveforms for changes in cardiovascular function.
Compare hemodynamic parameters with other clinical signs and symptoms.
Use a closed-system cardiac output setup.
Obtain cardiac output by administering cardiac output injections over 4 seconds and averaging three injections that are within 1 L of each other.

Monitor the pulmonary artery and systemic arterial waveform; if damping occurs, check tubing for kinks or air bubbles, check connections, aspirate clot from the catheter tip, gently flush the system, or assist in catheter repositioning.
Document pulmonary artery and systemic arterial waveforms.
Monitor peripheral perfusion distal to the catheter insertion site every 4 hours or as appropriate.
Monitor for dyspnea, fatigue, tachypnea, and orthopnea.
Monitor for pulmonary catheter advancement resulting in spontaneous wedge, and notify the physician if this occurs.
Avoid inflating the balloon more frequently than every 1 to 2 hours, or as appropriate.
Monitor for balloon rupture (e.g., assess for resistance when inflating the balloon and allow the balloon to deflate passively after obtaining pulmonary capillary/pulmonary artery wedge pressure).
Prevent air embolism (e.g., remove air bubbles from tubing; if balloon rupture is suspected, do not attempt to reinflate the balloon and close the portal).
Maintain sterility of portals.
Maintain a closed pressure system over portals, as appropriate.
Perform sterile dressing changes and site care as appropriate.
Inspect the insertion site for signs of bleeding or infection.
Change IV solution and tubing every 24 to 72 hours based on protocols.
Monitor laboratory results for possible catheter-induced infections.
Administer fluids and/or volume expanders to maintain hemodynamic parameters within the specified range.
Administer pharmacological agents to maintain hemodynamic parameters within the specified range.
Instruct the patient and family on the therapeutic use of hemodynamic monitoring catheters.
Instruct the patient on activity restrictions during catheter use.

definicao: Collection and analysis of patient data to prevent or minimize neurological complications.
atividades: Monitor level of consciousness.
Monitor level of orientation.
Monitor the Glasgow Coma Scale trend.
Monitor recent memory, attention, past memory, mood, affect, and behaviors.
Monitor sense of smell.
Monitor visual disturbances (e.g., diplopia, nystagmus, visual field changes, blurred vision, visual acuity).
Monitor pupil size, shape, symmetry, and reactivity.
Monitor extraocular muscles (EOMs) and gaze characteristics.
Monitor for doll's eye response.
Monitor corneal reflex.
Monitor facial symmetry.
Monitor cough and gag reflex.
Monitor tongue protrusion.
Monitor muscle tone, strength, gait, and proprioception, comparing both sides of the body simultaneously.
Monitor for pronator drift, comparing both sides of the body simultaneously.
Monitor hand grip strength, comparing both sides of the body simultaneously.
Monitor tremor, comparing both sides of the body simultaneously.
Monitor vital signs (e.g., temperature, blood pressure, pulse, respirations).
Monitor respiratory status (e.g., arterial blood gas levels, pulse oximetry, depth, pattern, rate, effort).
Monitor invasive hemodynamic parameters, as appropriate.
Monitor intracranial pressure (ICP) and cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP).
Note complaints of headache.
Monitor speech characteristics (e.g., fluency, presence of aphasias or word-finding difficulties).
Monitor response to stimuli (e.g., verbal, tactile, noxious).
Monitor discrimination between sharp and dull or hot and cold.
Monitor for numbness and tingling.
Monitor diaphoresis patterns.
Monitor Babinski response.
Monitor for Cushing's response (i.e., rising systolic pressure with widening pulse pressure and bradycardia).
Monitor for drainage on the craniotomy or laminectomy dressing.
Monitor medication response.
Consult with colleagues to confirm data, as appropriate.
Identify emerging patterns in the data.
Increase the frequency of neurological monitoring, as appropriate.
Avoid activities that increase intracranial pressure.
Space necessary nursing activities that increase intracranial pressure.
Notify the physician of changes in the patient's condition.
Institute emergency protocols if needed.
Institute institutional protocol for specific neurological condition (e.g., stroke, tumor, aneurysm, trauma), as needed.

definicao: Collection and analysis of patient data related to nutrient consumption.
atividades: Weigh the patient.
Monitor growth and development.
Obtain anthropometric measurements of body composition (i.e., body mass index, waist measurement, and skinfold measurements).
Monitor weight loss and gain trends (in pediatric patients, plot height and weight on a standardized growth chart).
Identify recent changes in body weight.
Determine the appropriate amount of weight gain during the antepartum period.
Monitor skin turgor and elasticity.
Identify skin abnormalities (e.g., excessive bruising, poor wound healing, and bleeding).
Identify hair abnormalities (e.g., dry, thin, thick, and brittle).
Monitor nausea and vomiting.
Identify abnormalities in bowel function (e.g., diarrhea, blood, mucus, and irregular or painful bowel movements).
Monitor dietary caloric intake.
Identify recent changes in appetite and activity.
Monitor the type and amount of usual exercise.
Discuss the role of social and emotional aspects of food consumption.

Determine eating patterns (e.g., food preferences and aversions, excessive fast food consumption, missed meals, rushing while eating, parent-child interaction during feeding, frequency and duration of infant feeding).
Monitor pallor, hyperemia, and moisture of conjunctival tissue.
Identify nail abnormalities (e.g., spoon-shaped, cracked, split, broken, fragile, and striated).
Perform swallowing assessment (e.g., motor function of facial, oral, and tongue muscles, swallowing and gag reflex).
Identify oral cavity abnormalities (e.g., inflammation; spongy, receding, or bleeding gums; dry or cracked lips, sores, dry scarlet tongue; and hyperemia and hypertrophy of papillae).
Monitor mental status (e.g., confusion, depression, and anxiety).
Identify musculoskeletal system abnormalities (e.g., muscle atrophy, joint pain, bone fractures, and poor posture).

Conduct laboratory tests to monitor outcomes (e.g., cholesterol, serum albumin, transferrin, prealbumin, 24-hour urine nitrogen, BUN, creatinine, hemoglobin, hematocrit, cellular immunity, lymphocyte count, and electrolyte levels).
Determine energy recommendations (e.g., nutritional recommendations) based on patient factors (e.g., age, weight, height, gender, and physical activity level).

Determine factors affecting food intake (e.g., knowledge, availability, and accessibility to quality food products in all food categories; religious and cultural influences: gender; ability to prepare food; isolation

definicao: Collection and analysis of patient data to ensure airway clearance and adequate gas exchange.
atividades: Monitor rate, rhythm, depth, and effort of respirations.
Observe chest movements, noting symmetry, use of accessory muscles, and retraction of supraclavicular and intercostal muscles.
Monitor for noisy respirations, such as stridor and snoring.
Monitor respiratory patterns (e.g., bradypnea, tachypnea, hyperventilation, Kussmaul respirations, Cheyne-Stokes respirations, apnea, Biot's respirations, ataxic patterns).
Monitor oxygen saturation levels continuously in sedated patients (e.g., SaO2, SvO2, SpO2), according to institutional policy and as indicated.

Provide continuous non-invasive oxygenation sensors (e.g., devices used on fingers, nose, forehead) with appropriate alarm systems in high-risk patients (e.g., morbidly obese, confirmed obstructive sleep apnea, history of respiratory problems requiring oxygen therapy, extremes of age) according to institutional policy and as indicated.
Palpate for symmetrical lung expansion.
Perform anterior and posterior chest percussion from apices to bases bilaterally.
Observe tracheal position.
Monitor for diaphragmatic muscle fatigue, as indicated by paradoxical movements.
Auscultate breath sounds, noting areas of decreased or absent ventilation and presence of adventitious sounds.
Determine the need for suctioning after auscultating crackles or rhonchi over the large airways.
Auscultate lung sounds after treatments to note results.

Monitor pulmonary function test (PFT) values, particularly vital capacity, maximum inspiratory force, forced expiratory volume in 1 second (FEV1), and the FEV1/FVC (forced vital capacity) ratio, as available.
Monitor mechanical ventilator readings, noting elevations in inspiratory pressures and decreases in tidal volume, as appropriate.
Monitor for restlessness, anxiety, and sensation of 'air hunger.'
Observe for changes in SaO2, SvO2, and end-tidal CO2, and changes in blood gas values, as appropriate.
Monitor the patient's ability to cough effectively.
Observe onset, characteristics, and duration of cough.
Monitor the patient's respiratory secretions.
Provide frequent and intermittent monitoring of respiratory status in at-risk patients (e.g., opioid therapy, newborns, mechanical ventilation, burns to face or chest, neuromuscular disorders).
Monitor for dyspnea and events that improve or worsen shortness of breath.
Monitor for snoring and voice changes hourly in patients with facial burns.
Monitor for crepitus, as appropriate.
Monitor chest radiograph reports.
Clear the airway using the chin lift or jaw thrust maneuver, as appropriate.
Place the patient in the lateral position, when indicated, to prevent aspiration; logroll if cervical injury is suspected.
Institute resuscitation efforts as needed.
Institute respiratory therapy treatments (e.g., nebulizer) as needed.

definicao: Use of music to help achieve specific behavior, feeling, or physiological change.
atividades: Define the specific behavior and/or physiological change desired (e.g., relaxation, stimulation, concentration, pain reduction).
Determine the individual's interest in music.
Identify the individual's musical preferences.
Inform the individual about the purpose of the music experience.
Choose music selections special to the individual's preferences.
Assist the individual in assuming a comfortable position.
Limit external stimuli (e.g., lights, sounds, visitors, phone calls) during the listening experience.
Ensure music tapes/CDs and equipment are available for the individual.
Ensure the tapes/CDs and equipment are in good working order.
Provide headphones, as indicated.
Ensure the volume is adequate but not too loud.
Avoid putting music on and leaving it for long periods.
Facilitate the individual's active participation (e.g., playing an instrument or singing) if desired and feasible within the environment.
Avoid stimulating music after acute head trauma.

definicao: Preparing the patient for a predictable developmental or situational crisis.
atividades: Assist the patient in identifying a possible future developmental or situational crisis and its effects on personal and family life.
Instruct on normal development and behavior, as appropriate.
Inform about realistic expectations related to patient behavior.
Assess the patient's usual methods of problem-solving.
Assist the patient in deciding how to solve the problem.
Assist the patient in deciding who will solve the problem.
Use case examples to strengthen the patient's problem-solving skills, as appropriate.
Assist the patient in identifying available resources and options for courses of action, as appropriate.
Practice techniques needed to cope with a future developmental or situational crisis, as appropriate.
Assist the patient in adapting to anticipated role changes.
Provide a ready reference for the patient (e.g., educational materials, handouts), as appropriate.
Suggest printed and electronic sources for the patient to read, as appropriate.
Refer the patient to community agencies, as appropriate.
Schedule visits at strategic developmental or situational periods.
Schedule extra visits for patients with concerns or difficulties.
Schedule follow-up phone calls to assess success or need for reinforcement.
Provide the patient with a telephone number to call for help if needed.
Include the family and other significant others when possible.

definicao: Assisting parents in understanding and helping their adolescent children.
atividades: Ask parents to describe the characteristics of their adolescent child.
Discuss the parent-child relationship during the previous school-age years.
Understand the relationship between parental behavior and age-appropriate goals for the child.

Identify personal factors impacting the success of the educational program (e.g., cultural values, presence of negative experiences with social workers, language barriers, time commitment, scheduling issues, travel, lack of general interest).

Identify the presence of family stressors (e.g., parental depression, substance dependence, alcoholism, low educational level, domestic violence, marital conflict, family blending after divorce, and excessive punishment of children).
Discuss how parents were disciplined when they were adolescents.
Instruct parents on normal physiological, emotional, and cognitive characteristics of adolescents.
Identify developmental challenges or goals of the adolescent life period.
Identify the most commonly used defense mechanisms by adolescents, such as denial and intellectualization.
Include the effects of the adolescent's cognitive development on information processing.
Include the effects of the adolescent's cognitive development on decision-making.
Ask parents to describe the discipline methods used before the child became an adolescent and whether parents consider them successful.
Provide online sources, books, and literature aimed at guiding parents on raising adolescents.
Describe the importance of power and control issues for parents and adolescents during the adolescent years.
Instruct parents on essential communication skills that will enhance their ability to build an empathetic relationship with their children and assist in problem-solving.
Instruct parents on methods of demonstrating their love for their adolescent children.
Explore parallels between adolescent children's dependence on their parents and on same-age social groups.
Reinforce the normalcy of the adolescent's vacillation between the desire for independence and regression to dependence.
Discuss the effects of separation between adolescents and their parents on marital relationships.
Share strategies for managing the adolescent's perception of parental rejection.
Facilitate expression of parents' feelings.
Assist parents in identifying reasons for their reactions to adolescents.
Identify ways to help the adolescent manage anger.
Instruct parents on how to use conflict for mutual understanding and family growth.
Create imaginary situations to stimulate family conflict management.
Discuss with parents issues they will agree to compromise on or not.
Discuss the need for and legitimacy of setting limits for adolescents.
Address strategies for setting limits for adolescents.
Instruct parents to use reality and consequences to manage adolescent behavior.
Refer parents to support groups or guidance as appropriate.

definicao: Guidance on nutrition and physical care during the first year of life.
atividades: Determine parents' knowledge, availability, and ability to learn about infant care.
Monitor the family's learning needs.
Provide anticipatory guidance on developmental changes during the first year of life.
Assist parents in developing ways to integrate the infant into the family system.
Teach parents the necessary skills for newborn care.
Instruct parents on bottle preparation and formula choices.
Provide parents with information on pacifiers.
Provide information on introducing solid foods into the diet during the first year.
Instruct parents on appropriate fluoride supplementation.
Provide information on teething development and oral hygiene during the first year.
Discuss alternatives to bottle feeding at bedtime to prevent tooth decay formation.
Provide anticipatory guidance on changes in elimination patterns during the first year.
Instruct parents on how to treat and prevent diaper rash.
Provide anticipatory guidance on changes in sleep patterns during the first year.
Demonstrate ways parents can stimulate infant development.
Encourage parents to hold, cuddle, massage, and touch the infant.
Encourage parents to talk and read to the infant.
Encourage parents to provide pleasant auditory and visual stimulation.
Encourage parents to play with the infant.
Give examples of safe toys or objects available at home that can be used as toys.
Encourage parents to participate in infant care classes.
Provide parents with written materials appropriate for identified learning needs.
Reinforce parents' ability to apply learning in child care.
Provide support to parents during learning for infant care.
Assist parents in interpreting the infant's cues, non-verbal cues, cries, and vocalizations.
Provide anticipatory guidance on changes in sleep patterns during the first year.
Provide information on newborn behavioral characteristics.
Demonstrate reflexes to parents and explain their meanings in infant care.
Discuss the infant's interaction capabilities.
Assist parents in identifying the infant's behavioral characteristics.
Demonstrate the infant's capabilities and strengths to parents.
Explain and demonstrate infant states.
Demonstrate techniques for calming the infant.
Monitor parents' skills in recognizing the infant's physiological needs.

definicao: Promoting the patient's awareness of personal identity, time, and space.
atividades: Address the patient by name when initiating an interaction.
Approach the patient slowly and from the front.
Use a calm, slow method when interacting with the patient.
Use a consistent approach (e.g., gentle firmness, active friendliness, passive friendliness, reality-based, and non-demanding) that reflects the patient's need and ability.
Speak distinctly with appropriate pace, volume, and tone.
Ask one question at a time.

Avoid frustrating the patient with demands that exceed their capacity (e.g., repeated orientation questions that cannot be answered, abstract thinking when the patient can only think concretely, activities that cannot be performed, decision-making beyond the patient's preference or ability).
Inform the patient about their orientation in time and space.
Present reality in a way that preserves patient dignity (e.g., provide an alternative explanation, avoid questioning and attempts to convince the patient).
Repeat the patient's last expressed thought, as appropriate.
Interrupt confabulation by changing the subject or responding to the feeling or theme rather than the content of the verbalization.
Give one instruction at a time.
Use gestures and objects to enhance understanding of verbal communications.
Engage the patient in concrete, immediate activities (e.g., activities of daily living) that focus on something outside the patient's mind and are concrete and reality-oriented.
Provide physical and postural assistance (e.g., move the patient's hands to help them brush their teeth), as needed to complete the task.
Encourage the use of aids that enhance sensory perception (e.g., glasses, hearing aids, and dentures).
Recommend that the patient wear their own clothing, assisting as needed.
Provide objects that symbolize gender identity (e.g., purse or cap), as appropriate.
Use visual cues to promote appropriate use of items.
Avoid unfamiliar situations when possible.
Prepare the patient for changes that will occur in the usual routine and environment before they happen.
Provide adequate rest and sleep, including short sleep intervals during the day as needed.
Provide caregivers who are familiar with the patient.
Encourage the family to participate in care based on the patient's abilities, needs, and preferences.
Provide a consistent physical environment and daily routine.
Provide access to familiar objects when possible.
Label items in the environment to promote recognition.
Modulate human and environmental sensory stimuli (e.g., visiting sessions, sensations, sounds, lighting, odors, and tactile stimuli) based on patient needs.
Use environmental cues (e.g., signs, pictures, clocks, calendars, and color-coded environmental schemes) to stimulate memory, reorient, and promote appropriate behavior.
Remove stimuli, when possible, that create misinterpretation for a particular patient (e.g., wall pictures and television).
Provide access to news of current events (e.g., television, newspapers, radio, verbal reports), as appropriate.
Involve the patient in a reality orientation group when appropriate and available.
Provide psychological education for the family and friends regarding promotion of reality orientation.
Monitor for changes in orientation, cognitive and behavioral functioning, and quality of life.

definicao: Facilitating patient and family location and appropriate use of health services.
atividades: Explain the healthcare system, how it works, and what the patient/family can expect.
Assist the patient or family in coordinating healthcare and communication.
Assist the patient or family in choosing appropriate healthcare professionals.

Instruct the patient on what type of services to expect from each healthcare professional (nurse specialists, registered dietitians, registered nurses, nursing technicians, physical therapists, cardiologists, internal medicine professionals, optometrists, and psychologists).

Inform the patient about different types of healthcare institutions (e.g., general hospital, specialized hospital, teaching hospital, outpatient clinic, and outpatient surgery clinic), as appropriate.
Inform the patient about accreditation and health department requirements for judging the quality of an institution.
Inform the patient about appropriate community resources and contact persons.
Advise the use of a second opinion.
Inform the patient of the right to change healthcare professionals.
Inform the patient about the meaning of signing a consent form.
Provide the patient with a copy of the Patient's Bill of Rights.
Instruct the patient on how to access emergency services by phone and vehicle, as appropriate.

definicao: Administration of oxygen and monitoring of its effectiveness.
atividades: Clear oral, nasal, and tracheal secretions, as appropriate.
Restrict smoking.
Maintain airway clearance.
Set up oxygen equipment and administer it via a heated, humidified system.
Administer supplemental oxygen as prescribed.
Monitor oxygen flow in liters.
Monitor the position of the oxygen delivery device.
Instruct the patient on the importance of leaving the oxygen delivery device on.
Periodically check the oxygen delivery device to ensure the prescribed concentration is being delivered.
Monitor the effectiveness of oxygen therapy (e.g., pulse oximetry, arterial blood gases), as appropriate.
Ensure replacement of the oxygen mask/cannula whenever the device is removed.
Monitor the patient's ability to tolerate oxygen removal while eating.
Change the oxygen delivery device from mask to nasal cannula during meals as tolerated.
Observe for signs of oxygen-induced hypoventilation.
Monitor for signs of oxygen toxicity and absorption atelectasis.
Monitor oxygen equipment to ensure it is not interfering with the patient's breathing attempts.
Monitor the patient's anxiety related to the need for oxygen therapy.
Monitor for skin breakdown from friction of the oxygen device.
Provide oxygen when the patient is transported.
Instruct the patient to obtain a prescription for supplemental oxygen before air travel or travel to high altitudes, as appropriate.
Consult other healthcare personnel about the use of supplemental oxygen during activity and/or sleep.
Instruct the patient and family on the use of oxygen at home.
Provide oxygen devices that facilitate mobility and teach the patient appropriately.
Alternate the oxygen delivery device to promote comfort, as appropriate.

definicao: Birth of a baby.
atividades: Provide anticipatory guidance for delivery.
Include support person(s) in the delivery experience, as appropriate.
Perform vaginal examination to determine fetal position and engagement.
Maintain discretion and privacy for the patient in a quiet environment during delivery.
Attend to the patient's requests regarding delivery management when consistent with perinatal care standards.
Obtain permission from the patient and partner(s) when other healthcare professionals enter the delivery room.
Assist the patient in positioning for delivery.
Inform the patient of the need for episiotomy.
Administer local anesthetic before delivery or episiotomy as indicated.
Perform episiotomy as appropriate.
Instruct the patient on shallow breathing (e.g., short, rapid breaths) with fetal crowning.
Guide the fetal head slowly, maintaining flexion until the parietal bones have delivered.
Support the perineum during delivery.

definicao: Exchange of essential patient care information with another nursing team during shift change or change in care setting.
atividades: Select the type of handoff (e.g., face-to-face, audio, at the patient's bedside, rounds, electronic health record summary, team meeting) to use.
Introduce yourself to the oncoming team as needed.
Review pertinent demographics, including name, age, and room number.
Identify the chief complaint, reason for admission, and recent surgeries, as appropriate.
Identify attending and consulting physicians.
Summarize relevant health history as needed.
Identify essential medical and nursing diagnoses, as appropriate.
Identify resolved medical and nursing diagnoses, as appropriate.
Present information succinctly, focusing on recent, significant data needed for the nursing team to assume care responsibility.
Encourage the patient to contribute to the handoff and ask questions if the handoff is conducted at the bedside.
Use a standardized handoff format, instrument, or mnemonic (e.g., SBAR: Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation).
Describe the treatment regimen, including diet, fluid therapy, medications, and exercise.
Review the tubes the patient has, where they are, when they were placed, and when containers need to be emptied, as appropriate.
Identify laboratory and diagnostic tests to be completed in the next 24 hours.
Review relevant recent laboratory and diagnostic test results, as appropriate.
Describe health status data, including vital signs and signs and symptoms present during shift change.
Describe nursing interventions being implemented.
Describe the patient's and family's response to nursing interventions.
Summarize progress toward goals.
Summarize discharge plans, as appropriate.
Train new nurses in the clinical context of the handoff process.
Periodically evaluate the handoff process and revise if indicated.

definicao: Institution of necessary dietary restrictions with subsequent diet progression as tolerated.
atividades: Check for the presence of bowel sounds.
Institute NPO status as needed.
Clamp the nasogastric tube and monitor tolerance, as appropriate.
Monitor alertness and presence of gag reflex, as appropriate.
Monitor tolerance to ice chips and water intake.
Check if the patient is passing flatus.
Collaborate with other healthcare team members to advance the diet as quickly as possible without complications.
Advance the diet from clear liquid, full liquid, light, regular, or special, as tolerated, for adults and children.
Advance from dextrose or oral electrolyte solution, low-iron formula to full-iron formulas for infants.
Monitor tolerance to diet progression.
Offer six small meals instead of three meals, as appropriate.
Find ways to include patient preferences in the diet.

definicao: Implementation of necessary dietary changes in progressive phases following bariatric surgery.
atividades: Implement fasting or only small amounts of water for the 24 to 48 hours following surgery, according to institutional policy.
Administer dextrose, saline, or lactated Ringer's solutions to provide adequate nutrition in the first 24 hours and until the patient tolerates a full liquid diet.
Progress to a liquid diet lasting 2 to 3 weeks.
Instruct the patient to take small amounts of room-temperature liquids (e.g., broths, unsweetened juice, milk) slowly, consuming between 60 and 90 mL at a time.
Instruct patients to keep unsweetened beverages with them and drink them frequently.
Incorporate pureed foods (e.g., broth blended with beans, well-cooked fish or ground meat; yogurt; blended fruits) into the diet once the patient's body is adjusted to liquids.
Add mashed solid foods (e.g., minced meats, preserved fruits, oatmeal, eggs) between 6 and 8 weeks after surgery.
Instruct the patient to eat protein foods first at meals.
Progress to firmer foods low in sugar, saturated and trans fats, and containing high-quality protein, maintaining this diet for life.
Encourage patients to eat breakfast and at least four to five small meals per day.
Instruct patients to take small bites, eat slowly, and chew well-cooked solids thoroughly.
Instruct patients to incorporate fresh fruits and vegetables into their fluid and food intake.
Monitor for lactose intolerance as a possible surgical complication.
Work with a dietitian after surgery to ensure optimal protein nutrition and modify the diet as needed.
Find ways to include patient preferences in the prescribed diet.
Make the meal environment as pleasant as possible.
Place dietary restrictions on the bedside, in the chart, and in the care plan.
Instruct the patient to avoid foods and beverages high in sugar (e.g., sodas, juices, milk shakes, regular ice cream), as they can cause dumping syndrome.
Instruct the patient to avoid fluid intake approximately half an hour before eating, during the meal, and half an hour after the meal to reduce the occurrence of vomiting and diarrhea.
Instruct the patient on the need to take adult multivitamins with iron, B complex, and calcium.
Monitor tolerance to diet progression.
Encourage patients to keep a record of the types and amounts of foods that cause discomfort, malaise, or intolerance.
Encourage patients to do at least 35 minutes of aerobic exercise daily with strength training 3 times per week to maintain a good metabolic rate.
Encourage attendance at support groups for several months after surgery.

definicao: Preparation for transfer of a patient from one level of care to another, within the same healthcare institution or to another setting.
atividades: Assist the patient/family/significant others in preparing for discharge.
Assess the patient's capabilities for discharge.
Collaborate with physician, patient, family, significant others, and other healthcare team members in planning for continuity of healthcare.
Coordinate efforts of different healthcare professionals to ensure timely discharge.
Identify the patient's and primary caregiver's understanding of knowledge or skills needed after discharge.
Identify patient teaching needs for post-discharge care.
Monitor readiness for discharge.
Communicate discharge plans to the patient, as appropriate.
Document patient discharge plans in the chart.
Formulate a maintenance plan for post-discharge follow-up.
Assist patient, family, and significant others in planning for the supportive environment needed to provide post-hospital care to the patient.
Develop a plan that considers the patient's healthcare, social, and financial needs.
Arrange post-discharge assessment, as appropriate.
Encourage self-care as appropriate.
Arrange discharge to the next level of care.
Arrange caregiver support as appropriate.
Discuss financial resources if needed to arrange healthcare after discharge.
Coordinate relevant referrals for connections between healthcare professionals.

definicao: Assisting the patient in determining and providing the method of pregnancy prevention.
atividades: Assess the patient's knowledge and understanding of contraceptive methods.
Instruct the patient on the physiology of human reproduction, including the male and female reproductive systems, as needed.
Conduct a relevant physical examination if indicated by history.

definicao: Facilitating decision-making regarding pregnancy.
